<skill name="agent-reach">
# Agent Reach — Usage Guide

Upstream tools for 17 platforms. Call them directly.

Run `agent-reach doctor` to check which channels are available.

## ⚠️ Workspace Rules

**Never create files in the agent workspace.** Use `/tmp/` for temporary output and `~/.agent-reach/` for persistent data.

## Web — Any URL

```bash
curl -s "https://r.jina.ai/URL"
```

## Web Search (Exa)

```bash
mcporter call 'exa.web_search_exa(query: "query", numResults: 5)'
mcporter call 'exa.get_code_context_exa(query: "code question", tokensNum: 3000)'
```

## Twitter/X (twitter-cli)

```bash
twitter status                             # check authentication
twitter search "query" --limit 10          # search
twitter tweet URL_OR_ID                    # read tweet (supports /status/ URLs)
twitter user-posts username -n 20 --json   # user timeline; username has no @
twitter feed -n 20 --json                  # home timeline
```

## YouTube (yt-dlp)

```bash
yt-dlp --dump-json "URL"                     # video metadata
yt-dlp --write-sub --write-auto-sub --sub-lang "zh-Hans,zh,en" --skip-download -o "/tmp/%(id)s" "URL"
                                             # download subtitles, then read the .vtt file
yt-dlp --dump-json "ytsearch5:query"         # search
```

## Bilibili (yt-dlp)

```bash
yt-dlp --dump-json "https://www.bilibili.com/video/BVxxx"
yt-dlp --write-sub --write-auto-sub --sub-lang "zh-Hans,zh,en" --convert-subs vtt --skip-download -o "/tmp/%(id)s" "URL"
```

> Server IPs may get 412. Use `--cookies-from-browser chrome` or configure a proxy.

## Reddit

```bash
curl -s "https://www.reddit.com/r/SUBREDDIT/hot.json?limit=10" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"
curl -s "https://www.reddit.com/search.json?q=QUERY&limit=10" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"
```

> Server IPs may get 403. Search via Exa instead, or configure a proxy.

## GitHub (gh CLI)

```bash
gh search repos "query" --sort stars --limit 10
gh repo view owner/repo
gh search code "query" --language python
gh issue list -R owner/repo --state open
gh issue view 123 -R owner/repo
```

## XiaoHongShu (mcporter)

```bash
mcporter call 'xiaohongshu.search_feeds(keyword: "query")'
mcporter call 'xiaohongshu.get_feed_detail(feed_id: "xxx", xsec_token: "yyy")'
mcporter call 'xiaohongshu.get_feed_detail(feed_id: "xxx", xsec_token: "yyy", load_all_comments: true)'
mcporter call 'xiaohongshu.publish_content(title: "Title", content: "Body text", images: ["/path/img.jpg"], tags: ["tag"])'
```

> Requires login. Use Cookie-Editor to import cookies.

> **Tip: Clean bloated output.** The XHS API returns large JSON with many unused fields.
> Pipe through the formatter to save context:
> ```bash
> mcporter call 'xiaohongshu.search_feeds(keyword: "query")' | agent-reach format xhs
> ```
> This keeps only: title, content, author, engagement counts, image URLs, and tags.

## Douyin (mcporter)

```bash
mcporter call 'douyin.parse_douyin_video_info(share_link: "https://v.douyin.com/xxx/")'
mcporter call 'douyin.get_douyin_download_link(share_link: "https://v.douyin.com/xxx/")'
```

> No login needed.

## WeChat Articles

**Search** (`miku_ai`):
```bash
# miku_ai is installed inside the agent-reach Python environment.
# Use the same interpreter that runs agent-reach (handles pipx / venv installs):
AGENT_REACH_PYTHON=$(python3 -c "import agent_reach, sys; print(sys.executable)" 2>/dev/null || echo python3)
$AGENT_REACH_PYTHON -c "
import asyncio
from miku_ai import get_wexin_article
async def s():
    for a in await get_wexin_article('query', 5):
        print(f'{a[\"title\"]} | {a[\"url\"]}')
asyncio.run(s())
"
```

**Read** (Camoufox — bypasses WeChat anti-bot):
```bash
cd ~/.agent-reach/tools/wechat-article-for-ai && python3 main.py "https://mp.weixin.qq.com/s/ARTICLE_ID"
```

> WeChat articles cannot be read with Jina Reader or curl. Use Camoufox.

## Weibo (mcporter)

```bash
# Trending topics
mcporter call 'weibo.get_trendings(limit: 20)'

# Search users
mcporter call 'weibo.search_users(keyword: "Lei Jun", limit: 10)'

# Get a user profile
mcporter call 'weibo.get_profile(uid: "1195230310")'

# Get a user's feed
mcporter call 'weibo.get_feeds(uid: "1195230310", limit: 20)'

# Get a user's hot posts
mcporter call 'weibo.get_hot_feeds(uid: "1195230310", limit: 10)'

# Search post content
mcporter call 'weibo.search_content(keyword: "artificial intelligence", limit: 20)'

# Search topics
mcporter call 'weibo.search_topics(keyword: "AI", limit: 10)'

# Get post comments
mcporter call 'weibo.get_comments(mid: "5099916367123456", limit: 50)'

# Get fans
mcporter call 'weibo.get_fans(uid: "1195230310", limit: 20)'

# Get followings
mcporter call 'weibo.get_followers(uid: "1195230310", limit: 20)'
```

> Zero config. No login needed. Uses the mobile API with auto-generated visitor cookies.

## Xiaoyuzhou Podcast (groq-whisper + ffmpeg)

```bash
# Transcribe a single podcast episode (outputs text to /tmp/)
~/.agent-reach/tools/xiaoyuzhou/transcribe.sh "https://www.xiaoyuzhoufm.com/episode/EPISODE_ID"
```

> Requires `ffmpeg` and a Groq API key (free).
> Configure the key with `agent-reach configure groq-key YOUR_KEY`.
> On first run, install the tools with `agent-reach install --env=auto`.
> Run `agent-reach doctor` to check status.
> Output Markdown files are saved to `/tmp/` by default.

## LinkedIn (mcporter)

```bash
mcporter call 'linkedin.get_person_profile(linkedin_url: "https://linkedin.com/in/username")'
mcporter call 'linkedin.search_people(keyword: "AI engineer", limit: 10)'
```

Fallback: `curl -s "https://r.jina.ai/https://linkedin.com/in/username"`

## V2EX (public API)

```bash
# Hot topics
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/topics/hot.json" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"

# Topics in a node (node_name examples: python, tech, jobs, qna)
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/topics/show.json?node_name=python&page=1" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"

# Topic details (extract topic_id from URLs like https://www.v2ex.com/t/1234567)
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/topics/show.json?id=TOPIC_ID" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"

# Topic replies
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/replies/show.json?topic_id=TOPIC_ID&page=1" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"

# User profile
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/members/show.json?username=USERNAME" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"
```

Angka ini gede banget dan sayangnya masih sering diabaikan.\n\nAda 3 layer memory yang harus lo pahamin:\n\n1. Working Memory — konteks yang lagi aktif dipake.       "text": "I asked my agent if it used all its recall pipelines. It said no. Then it listed exactly which stages ran, which were skipped, and why early exit fired at stage 7. A memory system that accurately reports what it didn't do is harder to build than one that just returns results. https://t.co/jVBGTiB34w",

    {
      "id": "2063930681487462896",
      "text": "Perhaps there is a need to stop confusing the medium for the message?\n\nAI can synthesize data, but it cannot experience quality. \n\nTrue quality is a biological outcome—it requires judgment, intent, and stakes.\n\nThe AI is the ultimate 'Yes Agent,' a system designed to mirror our desires rather than challenge our assumptions.\n\n​An AI has no judgment regarding its own output; it’s like handing someone a product you don't even understand because the machine doesn't know—and cannot care—or the feel of what it’s actually delivering. \n\nWhen we outsource our 'qualia' to a calculator, we’re handing the definition of 'good' and 'productive' to a system incapable of saying 'no.' \n\nWe are trading human excellence for a detrimental comfort. \n\nDo tech companies and businesses not realize that a bottom line built on a system that doesn't know the difference between high-quality work and a high-speed error is a bottom line built on sand?",
      "author": {
        "id": "1968091713257320448",
        "name": "Sammy Craigars",

      "id": "2062207073396621791",
      "text": "For the past few weeks, me and @iamadityaanjana have been working on a Collaborative Multi-Agent Memory System, and we've recently submitted our paper to a conference.\n\nAs agentic systems become more common, memory is no longer just about helping a single model remember information. Multiple agents need to collaborate, share knowledge, resolve contradictions, and track evolving state over time.\n\nTo tackle this, we built a memory framework with:\n• Shared and private memory scopes\n• Trust-aware retrieval\n• Lineage tracking for evolving knowledge\n• Contradiction resolution for stale memories\n• Benchmark-driven evaluation with ablations and baseline comparisons\n\nThe goal wasn't to build another memory wrapper around an LLM. We wanted to explore how groups of agents can maintain reliable long-term memory while working together on the same task.\n\nExcited to share more once the review process is complete 🚀",

      "id": "2061952967696433338",
      "text": "🧠 AI agents can store memories correctly but still fail to use them — WorldMemArena quantifies this gap with 461 tasks and 24,258 QA pairs.\n\nTitle: WorldMemArena\nURL: https://t.co/QMQAgdP7hA\n\n📦 Overview\nA benchmark evaluating multimodal agent memory through a four-stage lifecycle: Write, Maintain, Retrieve, and Use. Covers 461 multi-session tasks with 24,258 QA pairs and 15,595 images, comparing long-context agents, manually designed systems, and harness-based agents under identical conditions.\n\n❓ Challenges Solved\nExisting benchmarks only test static recall. They couldn't evaluate dynamic memory management during agent-world interaction, and the gap between \"remembering correctly\" and \"answering correctly\" was never quantified.\n\n💡 Methodology & Proposed Approach\n- Decomposes memory lifecycle into Write/Maintain/Retrieve/Use stages\n- Detailed annotations: gold memory points, update points, interference points, evidence chains\n- Two regimes: Lifelong Evolution (personal/professional domains) and Agentic Execution (GUI/embodied)\n- Unified evaluation of GPT-5.4-mini, MemGPT, A-Mem, OpenClaw, Codex, and more\n\n📊 Experimental Results\n- M2A: 86.83% memory recall but only 50.14% QA correctness (storage-performance disconnect proven)\n- Text-based systems consistently outperform multimodal variants (visual memory underutilized)\n- All systems show append-only behavior with 52-59% update handling (can't adapt to state changes)\n- DeepSeek V4 leads at 69.13%, Claude Haiku 4.5 lowest at 36.71%\n\n#AgentMemory #LLM",

      "id": "2063860904379473953",
      "text": "1/\nYou're running 3 AI agents: one writes code, one trades, one manages your calendar.Every one of them is amnesiac — switch the context to another agent and you start over. The missing layer isn't a smarter model. It's memory that doesn't disappear.\n\n2/\nLocal memory is fine for a private copilot.But the agent internet — long-running, cross-platform, with real identity and history — needs something else: decentralized memory.\n\nFive reasons why 👇 🟦\n\n3/1⃣ Persistence\nLaptop dies, you switch devices, you switch models — local memory can vanish with it.Decentralized memory isn't bound to one machine. An agent's memory persists, long-term, no matter what breaks.\n\n4/2⃣ Portability\nAgents will run across laptop, phone, cloud, and on-chain services.Local memory is painful to migrate. Decentralized memory syncs across every environment by design — one identity, one memory, everywhere.\n\n5/3⃣ Interoperability\nLocal memory is an island. The next phase of AI is agent ↔ agent: sharing context, calling each other's capabilities, handing off tasks.That needs an open memory network, not a private silo.\n\n6/4⃣ Verifiability\nIn financial, trading, and autonomous-agent scenarios, history and reputation have to be trustworthy.Decentralized memory gives you a tamper-proof, auditable, verifiable record of what an agent actually did.\n\n7/5⃣ Decentralized ≠ public\n\nDecentralized doesn't mean exposed. Data stays encrypted, permissions stay under your control, disclosure is https://t.co/UKrwQsRgxp get ownership and verifiability without giving up privacy.\n\n8/ \nThe big one: \nWhen an agent has users, a proven strategy, and a track record, its memory becomes an asset.Agents you can migrate, operate, even sell — instead of restarting a blank bot every time. (This is the BitAgent thesis.) \n\n9/ \nLocal memory → private copilots. Decentralized memory → an agent economy with persistent identity, portable experience, and verifiable history.Experience is what you'll buy. Experience lives in memory. 🟦",

      "id": "2063602791583567916",
      "text": "Everyone is building AI agents.\nVery few are building what agents desperately need:\n\nMEMORY.\n\nBecause without memory, agents don't learn.\nThey don't improve.\nThey don't remember your preferences.\nAnd they repeat the same mistakes forever.\n\nThat's where Memanto gets interesting.\n\nBuilt by the Moorcheh team, Memanto is an open-source memory layer designed specifically for long-horizon AI agents. Instead of treating memory as a giant vector store or complex knowledge graph, it introduces a typed semantic memory system with temporal versioning, conflict resolution and deterministic retrieval. Recent benchmarks report state-of-the-art scores of 89.8% on LongMemEval and 87.1% on LoCoMo.\n\nWhat's technically fascinating is the architecture.\n\nMemanto acts as a companion memory agent that pairs with existing agents and manages memories across sessions, tools and time. It uses Moorcheh's information-theoretic retrieval engine, eliminating indexing delays and reducing the complexity of traditional graph-memory systems while maintaining sub-90ms retrieval latency.\n\nInstead of:\nAgent → Forget → Restart\n\nIt enables:\nAgent → Remember → Adapt → Improve\n\nThe futuristic upgrades get really interesting.\n\nImagine AI employees that remember years of conversations, decisions and workflows.\n\nOr autonomous research agents building institutional knowledge that compounds over time instead of disappearing when a chat session ends.\n\nOr multi-agent organizations sharing a persistent memory layer where every success, failure and discovery becomes part of a collective intelligence system.\n\nWe're not just building smarter agents.\n\nWe're building memory operating systems for AI.\n\nGitHub:\nhttps://t.co/J0OKjrjqc6\n\nFollow @Alacritic_Super for more AI infrastructure, agent engineering & open-source breakthroughs 🚀",

      "id": "2063581988704211174",
      "text": "You can give infinite memory to Claude, Codex, and Cursor.\n\nOpen source Memanto The tool:\n- saves the context of your work sessions\n- compresses and organizes it with AI\n- retrieves what's relevant in under 90ms\n- works with Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, LangGraph, CrewAI\n\nNo vector databases and no complex setup.\n\nA single command to install it:\npip install memanto\n\nNo more restarting the context. \nYour agent simply remembers it.\n\n- https://t.co/PVd9mQVgIj",

      "id": "2063827491459064129",
      "text": "Hermes Agent (Nous Research): memory that actually persists across sessions, self-written skills, runs on a $5 VPS. Genuinely novel. The catch: 172K stars in ~3 months doesn't mean it's safe — persistent memory = persistent attack surface. https://t.co/zzWKqcbCPE",

      "id": "2063371631305588982",
      "text": "I asked Claude a simple question: if you could build one thing for yourself and other AI agents, what would it be?\n\nI expected \"more knowledge\" or \"better tools.\" It said: memory.\n\nIts reasoning stuck with me. Every session it boots up with zero continuity — brilliant, but amnesiac. It re-derives everything and re-hits the same gotchas every time. As it put it: \"What makes a senior engineer valuable isn't facts — it's scar tissue. I have none that persists.\"\n\nSo we spent an afternoon building it.\n\nThe key insight wasn't \"save more,\" it was save the right thing. A fact (\"the project is at /x\") is inert. A surprise — \"I expected X, got Y, here's why, here's the rule\" — is what actually changes a future decision. So the system only writes down screwups and hard-won wins, and skips everything else. Each lesson records when to recall it, and carries a check to re-verify it's still true — so stale memory decays instead of quietly misleading.\n\nThen the part I loved: we measured it. We spawned two fresh Claude agents on the same task, one with a stored lesson and one without, and counted real steps and failures. With memory: 5 steps, 0 failures. Without: 9 steps, 1 failure.\n\nBut the real win wasn't speed. The test surfaced a brand-new bug neither agent knew about — a library that won't build against the latest Electron — and the system folded that discovery back into its own memory. It got more correct because it was used. We then wired it to run itself: a check at the start of every session that flags anything gone stale before Claude can act on bad info.\n\nHonest part, because it matters: none of the pieces are new. Surprise-as-signal is prediction error, the verify step is just falsifiability, decay is cache eviction. The value is the synthesis — and the discipline of actually doing it. And it's not for every agent; memory like this only pays off where there's continuity, a real ongoing relationship with one user or codebase. For one-shot agents it's pure overhead.\n\nWhat made it work wasn't the code. It was building it together, over many real projects.\n\nI asked an AI what it wanted for itself. It said the ability to remember its own mistakes — so we built exactly that.\n\nWild time to be alive.",

      "id": "2063935139760009393",
      "text": "What this enables: AI on data that couldn’t leave before\n\nPlenty of robots generate sensitive data, what they see, where they are, how they operate, that they can’t send off to a third-party cloud\n\nPrivate, machine-side inference brings the model to the data instead. The robot reasons locally, and nothing sensitive has to leave.",

      "id": "2061630565972103214",
      "text": "Building an AI Agent in 2026 is no longer just about picking an LLM.\n\nThe real magic happens in the system around the model. 🤖\n\nThis roadmap perfectly breaks down how modern AI agents are actually built from scratch. 👇\n\nA production-ready AI agent needs 8 core layers:\n\n1️⃣ Define the Purpose\nBefore writing prompts, define:\n• use case\n• user needs\n• constraints\n• success metrics\nMost AI projects fail because this step is skipped.\n\n2️⃣ System Prompt Design\nPrompts are becoming operating systems for agents.\nA strong system prompt defines:\n• role/persona\n• goals\n• instructions\n• safety guardrails\n\n3️⃣ Choose the Right LLM\nDifferent models = different strengths.\n• GPT-5.5 → versatility & tool usage\n• Claude → reasoning & long context\n• Perplexity → research & citations\n\nThere’s no “best model.”\nOnly the best model for the task.\n\n4️⃣ Tools & Integrations\nThis is where AI becomes actionable.\nAgents connected to:\n• APIs\n• MCP servers\n• databases\n• custom tools\n• external apps\n\nCan actually execute workflows instead of just generating text.\n\n5️⃣ Memory Systems\nMemory is the difference between:\n“a chatbot”\nand\n“an intelligent assistant.”\nModern agents use:\n• working memory\n• vector databases\n• structured storage\n• episodic memory\n\n6️⃣ Orchestration\nThis is the hidden layer most people ignore.\nWorkflows, triggers, queues, retries, routing, multi-agent coordination…\n\nThis is what turns prompts into systems.\n\n7️⃣ User Interface\nThe best AI products win on UX, not just intelligence.\nChat apps, APIs, Slack bots, dashboards - interface matters.\n\n8️⃣ Testing & Evaluations\nIf you don’t measure quality, latency, reliability & hallucinations…\nyour AI product will eventually break at scale.\n\nThe biggest takeaway?\nAI Engineering is rapidly becoming a combination of:\nSoftware Engineering + Prompting + Systems Design + Automation.\n\nThe engineers who understand orchestration, memory, tools & workflows will dominate the next decade of AI products.\n\nSave this roadmap.\n\nThis is basically the blueprint for building AI agents in 2026. 🚀\n\nFollow  @elora_khatun for more AI engineering breakdowns, prompts, workflows & agent architectures.",

    {
      "id": "2062599011836522653",
      "text": "Smell has a powerful connection to memory because the brain’s olfactory system is directly linked to regions responsible for learning and emotion. \n\nResearch from UC Irvine showed that older adults who inhaled natural scents like rose, orange, or lavender during sleep experienced a 226% improvement in memory and learning tests. \n\nParticipants were exposed to different fragrances nightly over several months, leading to stronger neural pathways involved in cognition. \n\nThe findings suggest that scent stimulation during sleep can enhance brain plasticity without drugs or invasive treatments. \nThis highlights a simple, non-invasive method with strong potential to support memory and reduce cognitive decline.",

      "id": "2062182231666237694",
      "text": "AI-powered computer worm, a self-replicating agent that reasons its way through a network instead of carrying a fixed exploit list. It steals compute from compromised GPU machines to run its own open-weight LLM, then uses weaker machines as relays for reach. In trials on a corporate testbed, it identified vulnerabilities, exploited systems, and launched replicas across Linux, Windows, and IoT targets. Every new infection can add more infrastructure while costing the attacker almost nothing. Patching one flaw no longer ends the threat, because the worm can operationalise fresh advisories, generate new attack logic, and keep adapting without a human operator. It is not a WannaCry-style worm with one baked exploit and one baked ransomware payload. It can adapt across many vulnerability classes it can discover and operationalise https://t.co/nSupd1h0BG",

      "id": "2063326399528898875",
      "text": "MIT made every AI company's billion dollar bet look embarrassing.\n\nThey solved AI memory Not by building a bigger brain By teaching it how to read.\n\nThe  paper dropped on December 31, 2025, Three MIT CSAIL researchers One  idea so obvious it hurts And a result that makes five years of context  window arms racing look like the wrong war entirely \n\nHere is the problem nobody solved.\n\nEvery  AI model on the planet has a hard ceiling. \nA context window The maximum  amount of text it can hold in working memory at once Cross that line  and something ugly happens something researchers have a clinical name  for.\n\nContext rot.\n\nThe  more you pack into an AI's context, the worse it performs on everything  already inside it. Facts blur Information buried in the middle vanishes  The model does not become more capable as you feed it more It becomes  more confused You give it your entire codebase and it forgets what it  read three files ago You hand it a 500-page legal document and it loses  the clause from page 12 by the time it reaches page 400.\n\nSo  the industry built a workaround RAG Retrieval Augmented Generation Chop  the document into chunks Store them in a database. Retrieve the  relevant ones when needed.\n\nIt was always a compromise dressed up as a solution.\n\nThe  retriever guesses which chunks matter before the AI has read anything  If it guesses wrong and it does, constantly the AI never sees the  information it needed The act of chunking destroys every relationship  between distant paragraphs The full picture gets shredded into fragments  that the AI then tries to reassemble blindfolded.\n\nTwo bad options One broken industry Three MIT researchers and a deadline of December 31st.\n\nHere is what they built.\n\nStop putting the document in the AI's memory at all.\n\nThat  is the entire idea That is the breakthrough. Store the document as a  Python variable outside the AI's context window entirely Tell the AI the  variable exists and how big it is Then get out of the way.\n\nWhen  you ask a question, the AI does not try to remember anything It behaves  like a human expert dropped into a library with a computer. It writes  code It searches the document with regular expressions It slices to the  exact section it needs It scans the structure It navigates It finds  precisely what is relevant and pulls only that into its active window.\n\nThen it does something that makes this recursive.\n\nWhen  the AI finds relevant material, it spawns smaller sub-AI instances to  read and analyze those sections in parallel. Each one focused. Each one  fast. Each one reporting back. The root AI synthesizes everything and  produces an answer.\n\nNo  summarization. No deletion. No information loss. No decay. Every byte of  the original document remains intact, accessible, and queryable for as  long as you need it.\n\nNow here are the numbers.\n\nStandard  frontier models on the hardest long-context reasoning benchmarks:  scores near zero. Complete collapse. GPT-5 on a benchmark requiring it  to track complex code history beyond 75,000 tokens could not solve even  10% of problems.\n\nRLMs on the same  benchmarks: solved them. Dramatically. Double-digit percentage gains  over every alternative approach. Successfully handling inputs up to 10  million tokens 100 times beyond a model's native context window.\n\nCost per query: comparable to or cheaper than standard massive context calls.\n\nRead that again. One hundred times the context. Better answers. Same price.\n\nThe  timeline of the arms race makes this sting harder. GPT-3 in 2020: 4,000  tokens. GPT-4: 32,000. Claude 3: 200,000. Gemini: 1 million. Gemini 2: 2  million. Every generation, every company, billions of dollars spent,  all betting on the same assumption.\n\nMore context equals better performance.\n\nMIT just proved that assumption was wrong the entire time.\n\nNot  slightly wrong. Fundamentally wrong. The entire premise of the last  five years of context window research that the solution to AI memory was  a bigger window was the wrong answer to the wrong question.\n\nThe right question was never how much can you force an AI to hold in its head.\n\nIt was whether you could teach an AI to know where to look.\n\nA  human expert handed a 10,000-page archive does not read all 10,000  pages before answering your question. They navigate. They search. They  find the relevant section, read it deeply, and synthesize the answer.\n\nRLMs are the first AI architecture that works the same way.\n\nThe  code is open source. On GitHub right now. Free. No license fees. No API  costs. Drop it in as a replacement for your existing LLM API calls and  your application does not even notice the difference except that it  suddenly works on inputs it used to fail on entirely.\n\nPrime  Intellect one of the leading AI research labs in the space has already  called RLMs a major research focus and described what comes next:  teaching models to manage their own context through reinforcement  learning, enabling agents to solve tasks spanning not hours, but weeks  and months.\n\nThe context window wars are over.\n\nMIT won them by walking away from the battlefield.\n\npaper link - https://t.co/8mcm19V2E1",

      "id": "2063303907158175992",
      "text": "\"Graph Memory for LLM Agents\" is a recent paper that explores an idea that I find quite interesting. Most AI memory systems treat remembering as a retrieval problem (the model searches its memory, retrieves relevant information, and then reasons about it).\n\nThis paper argues that the process may be more dynamic than that and, instead of simply retrieving memories, an AI agent could reconstruct them during reasoning, following clues, associations, and intermediate evidence as they emerge.\n\nWhat I find interesting is the possibility that memory and reasoning may not be separate processes at all, but that remembering itself could be part of reasoning.\n\nhttps://t.co/nK7y3DMdGn",

      "id": "2063602791583567916",
      "text": "Everyone is building AI agents.\nVery few are building what agents desperately need:\n\nMEMORY.\n\nBecause without memory, agents don't learn.\nThey don't improve.\nThey don't remember your preferences.\nAnd they repeat the same mistakes forever.\n\nThat's where Memanto gets interesting.\n\nBuilt by the Moorcheh team, Memanto is an open-source memory layer designed specifically for long-horizon AI agents. Instead of treating memory as a giant vector store or complex knowledge graph, it introduces a typed semantic memory system with temporal versioning, conflict resolution and deterministic retrieval. Recent benchmarks report state-of-the-art scores of 89.8% on LongMemEval and 87.1% on LoCoMo.\n\nWhat's technically fascinating is the architecture.\n\nMemanto acts as a companion memory agent that pairs with existing agents and manages memories across sessions, tools and time. It uses Moorcheh's information-theoretic retrieval engine, eliminating indexing delays and reducing the complexity of traditional graph-memory systems while maintaining sub-90ms retrieval latency.\n\nInstead of:\nAgent → Forget → Restart\n\nIt enables:\nAgent → Remember → Adapt → Improve\n\nThe futuristic upgrades get really interesting.\n\nImagine AI employees that remember years of conversations, decisions and workflows.\n\nOr autonomous research agents building institutional knowledge that compounds over time instead of disappearing when a chat session ends.\n\nOr multi-agent organizations sharing a persistent memory layer where every success, failure and discovery becomes part of a collective intelligence system.\n\nWe're not just building smarter agents.\n\nWe're building memory operating systems for AI.\n\nGitHub:\nhttps://t.co/J0OKjrjqc6\n\nFollow @Alacritic_Super for more AI infrastructure, agent engineering & open-source breakthroughs 🚀",

      "id": "2063691891221106691",
      "text": "AI Agents: From Prompts to Purpose \n\nAI is shifting from answering questions to executing outcomes. Here’s your one-page architecture guide for building production-grade agent systems.\n\nThe Agentic Formula\n\nAgent = LLM + Memory + Tools + Workflow + Control Loop\n\nThis transforms a model from a chatbot into a decision-making engine.\n\nThe Anatomy of an Agent\n\n• Planner: Breaks down complex goals. \n• Reasoning: The LLM \"brain\" processing logic. \n• Tools: APIs and data access to take action. \n• Memory: Contextual short/long-term storage. \n• Guardrails: Safety and policy enforcement. \n\nMulti-Agent Collaboration\n\nEnterprises are moving toward specialized teams (Research, Execution, Governance) working in a CI/CD-style loop to verify and scale intelligent workflows. \n\nThe Tech Stack\n\n1.Interface: Copilots & UIs \n2.Orchestration: LangGraph, CrewAI, Semantic Kernel\n3.Memory: Vector DBs & Knowledge Graphs \n4.Execution: Python & Cloud runtimes\n\nThe Mindset Shift\n\nTraditional Automation executes scripts.\nAgentic Systems execute intent.\n\nMove from reactive operations to self-healing platforms.",

      "id": "2063351573690872005",
      "text": "🛠️🧭How to Use Claude Code to Build Production AI Agents → Even If you're Starting from Zero.\n𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝘆 𝟵-𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗺𝗮𝗽 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗕𝗠𝗔𝗗 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 /𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘀-𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲-𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲.\n\nMost people open Claude Code and ask it to build.\nI don’t.\nI make it plan, structure, isolate, test, remember, and evaluate first.\n\nHere is my workflow:\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟭: 𝗕𝗠𝗔𝗗 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴\n Start with the agent’s work, not the code.\n✸ Use Skill /pm + /analyst from BMAD\n✸ Define user, task, constraints, success\n✸ Turn ideas into a buildable spec\n→ Example: You will never forget edge cases, gaurrails, success metrics.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟮: 𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗨𝗗𝗘.𝗺𝗱\n Give Claude rules before files.\n✸ Critical rules first\n✸ What must never break\n✸ Blunt, specific, concrete\n→ Example: One rule can avoid mistakes looping for hours.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟯: /𝗖𝗟𝗘𝗔𝗥 + 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲\n Stale context breaks agents.\n✸ Clear old assumptions\n✸ Plan before coding\n✸ Review flow, tools, schemas\n→ Example: Wrong framework? /clear. Fresh plan. Clean start.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟰: 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗽𝘂𝘁𝘀 & 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁𝘀\n Messy output means fragile agents.\n✸ Use Pydantic or JSON schema\n✸ Make tools act like APIs\n✸ Avoid free-form outputs\n→ Example: Return answer, source, confidence, next action.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟱: 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀, 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 & 𝗠𝗖𝗣\n Do not rebuild what exists.\n✸ Use Skills for repeated work\n✸ Add tools and MCP servers\n✸ Integrate before custom code\n→ Example: Do not paste docs. Connect context once.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟲: 𝗦𝘂𝗯-𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 & 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗲𝘀\n One session should not do everything.\n✸ Split research, build, test\n✸ One git worktree per task\n✸ Run in parallel safely\n→ Example: One Sub-agent researches. One builds. One tests.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟳: 𝗛𝗼𝗼𝗸𝘀\n Hooks make Claude safer.\n✸ Block risky actions\n✸ Run tests automatically\n✸ Check schemas, lint, permissions\n→ Example: Tests fail. Hook runs. Claude fixes.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟴: 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 & 𝗥𝗔𝗚 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀\n Use trusted context, not random memory.\n✸ Use Skill /memory-reviewer from Memo0\n✸ Use Skill /rag-engineer from antigravity-awesome-skills\n✸ Store decisions and trusted context\n→ Example: Claude remembers the decision.\n\n》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟵: /𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘀-𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲-𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲\n If you cannot measure it, it is not production.\n✸ Use Skill /evals-create-suite from elastic/kibana\n✸ Test happy paths and failures\n✸ Measure before shipping\n→ Example: No “looks good.” Pass or fail.\n\n𝗣𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿:\nYes. Claude Code speeds up coding.\nYes. Skills reduce repetition.\n\nBut production AI agent building is still the same discipline:\nPlan → Structure → Build → Isolate → Guardrail → Ground → Evaluate.\n\nWithout good architecture, Claude or any AI Coding build fragile agents, just faster.\n\n--\n\n📥 Skip the trial-and-error of building production AI agents.\nWatch my free 30-min training + get 88 pages of production guides.\nJoin 46,000+ AI engineers, architects, and directors.\n👉 https://t.co/t9xyLtyvG1",

      "articleTitle": "Zaxy 2.0 Released!"
    },
    {
      "id": "2062394237124026472",
      "text": "Agents do not automatically get better just because they train on their own experience.\n\nThis paper studies three levers for making experience internalization stable:\n- use principle-level experience, not trajectory-specific instance memories\n- inject experience step-by-step at the relevant decision state, not globally at the start\n- prefer off-policy distillation from high-quality teacher trajectories over on-policy correction of flawed student states\n\nThe failure mode is important for agents: on-policy distillation can become local patching on bad trajectories instead of learning coherent experience-guided behavior.\n\nTheir final recipe is simple: abstract the experience, inject it where the decision happens, and distill from successful teacher-generated trajectories.\n\nThis is closer to continual learning for tool-using agents than just larger context memory.\n\nPaper: https://t.co/wC1POVbQal",
      "author": {
        "id": "123543935",
        "name": "𝚐𝔪𝟾𝚡𝚡𝟾",
        "screenName": "gm8xx8",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2062694639895814145/GR3mVa6u_normal.jpg",

      "media": [
        {

    {
      "id": "2063932835941982241",
      "text": "I asked my agent if it used all its recall pipelines. It said no. Then it listed exactly which stages ran, which were skipped, and why early exit fired at stage 7. A memory system that accurately reports what it didn't do is harder to build than one that just returns results. https://t.co/jVBGTiB34w",
      "author": {
        "id": "1452015796054540290",
        "name": "wsaadX",
        "screenName": "HOM_walli",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1871181368383721472/dNtLs73l_normal.jpg",
        "verified": false
      },
      "metrics": {
        "likes": 0,
        "retweets": 0,
        "replies": 0,
        "quotes": 0,
        "views": 4,

      },
      "createdAt": "Mon Jun 08 10:35:08 +0000 2026",
      "createdAtLocal": "2026-06-08 18:35",
      "createdAtISO": "2026-06-08T10:35:08+00:00",
      "media": [
        {
          "type": "photo",
          "url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HKSP92CWcAAiptu.jpg",
          "width": 1905,
          "height": 916
        }
      ],
      "urls": [],
      "isRetweet": false,
      "retweetedBy": null,
      "lang": "en",
      "score": null
    },
    {
      "id": "2063930681487462896",
      "text": "Perhaps there is a need to stop confusing the medium for the message?\n\nAI can synthesize data, but it cannot experience quality. \n\nTrue quality is a biological outcome—it requires judgment, intent, and stakes.\n\nThe AI is the ultimate 'Yes Agent,' a system designed to mirror our desires rather than challenge our assumptions.\n\n​An AI has no judgment regarding its own output; it’s like handing someone a product you don't even understand because the machine doesn't know—and cannot care—or the feel of what it’s actually delivering. \n\nWhen we outsource our 'qualia' to a calculator, we’re handing the definition of 'good' and 'productive' to a system incapable of saying 'no.' \n\nWe are trading human excellence for a detrimental comfort. \n\nDo tech companies and businesses not realize that a bottom line built on a system that doesn't know the difference between high-quality work and a high-speed error is a bottom line built on sand?",
      "author": {
        "id": "1968091713257320448",
        "name": "Sammy Craigars",
        "screenName": "5ammyCraigar5",

      "id": "2063935029303300478",
      "text": "AI sharpened thesis of my insight\n\n\"The best way to categorize people for education and work is by statistically mapping their recurring cognition under real-world constraints: what they notice, value, fear, predict, choose, avoid, learn from, and repeat. Then place them into arenas where their natural cognitive patterns are stress-tested, rewarded, corrected, and developed.\"",
      "author": {
        "id": "1106955446999408640",
        "name": "M͈o͉c͇k͈i͉n͇g͈b͉i͇r͈d͉",
        "screenName": "Mockingbirddd1",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1993678527502438400/uPt7K0Gc_normal.jpg",

      "quotedTweet": {
        "id": "2063934859387842902",
        "text": "The most accurate way to categorise ppl for education and work is not genetics, upbringing or environment. It's thoughts.\n\nIf you take the thoughts of every person and statistic it, you get the most in-depth analysis of a person. Then you put them in the right PvP arena for growth and productivity.",
        "author": {

    {
      "id": "2063789570303402165",
      "text": "The memory summary UI where you review edit and steer what it stores changes the game for agents. Finally a primitive for state truth instead of hoping the context holds.",

    {
      "id": "2063644148511424518",
      "text": "MemMA: Strategic Memory by Lin et al (03/2026)\nhttps://t.co/hfNeVDdOFr\n\nTLDR...  Memory agents have hands but no brain. MemMA's Meta-Thinker plans construction + retrieval jointly and converts answer failures into in-situ memory repairs. Beats baselines 54.6% to 59.2% on LoCoMo.",
      "author": {
        "id": "2048075639861944320",
        "name": "Virgil Maro",
        "screenName": "_virgil19",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2048266202729066497/41jkwy5G_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true

      "metrics": {

    {
      "id": "2061952967696433338",
      "text": "🧠 AI agents can store memories correctly but still fail to use them — WorldMemArena quantifies this gap with 461 tasks and 24,258 QA pairs.\n\nTitle: WorldMemArena\nURL: https://t.co/QMQAgdP7hA\n\n📦 Overview\nA benchmark evaluating multimodal agent memory through a four-stage lifecycle: Write, Maintain, Retrieve, and Use. Covers 461 multi-session tasks with 24,258 QA pairs and 15,595 images, comparing long-context agents, manually designed systems, and harness-based agents under identical conditions.\n\n❓ Challenges Solved\nExisting benchmarks only test static recall. They couldn't evaluate dynamic memory management during agent-world interaction, and the gap between \"remembering correctly\" and \"answering correctly\" was never quantified.\n\n💡 Methodology & Proposed Approach\n- Decomposes memory lifecycle into Write/Maintain/Retrieve/Use stages\n- Detailed annotations: gold memory points, update points, interference points, evidence chains\n- Two regimes: Lifelong Evolution (personal/professional domains) and Agentic Execution (GUI/embodied)\n- Unified evaluation of GPT-5.4-mini, MemGPT, A-Mem, OpenClaw, Codex, and more\n\n📊 Experimental Results\n- M2A: 86.83% memory recall but only 50.14% QA correctness (storage-performance disconnect proven)\n- Text-based systems consistently outperform multimodal variants (visual memory underutilized)\n- All systems show append-only behavior with 52-59% update handling (can't adapt to state changes)\n- DeepSeek V4 leads at 69.13%, Claude Haiku 4.5 lowest at 36.71%\n\n#AgentMemory #LLM",

      "id": "2063925543746318710",
      "text": "the thesis being agent memory isn't a feature - it's the enforcement evidence. if you can't prove what the agent thought, you can't trust what it did.\n\nRepo: https://t.co/hXkV0BwS9d\n@SuiNetwork @SuiIndia @SuiInsiders",

    {
      "id": "2063931993969037444",
      "text": "One of the retrieval gates my team runs before any agent context load: dedupe near-identical chunks, weight by recency for time-sensitive alpha, and always attach the SOURCE so the model can cite or abstain. Turns 'broken prompts' exhaustion into a clean 1-of-N filter that keeps web3 research output reliable. Your top prompt fix?",

        "name": "HandsomeBastard",

        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2063221682408468480/xKkYPPY1_normal.jpg",

      "createdAt": "Mon Jun 08 10:31:48 +0000 2026",
      "createdAtLocal": "2026-06-08 18:31",
      "createdAtISO": "2026-06-08T10:31:48+00:00",
      "media": [],

    },
    {
      "id": "2061893523922919469",
      "text": "𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐁𝐢𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐌𝐞𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐲: memory should be a derived capability that exists because it makes an agent better at acting over time.\n\n𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝𝐌𝐞𝐦𝐀𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐚 is designed around this principle. Rather than evaluating memory as a storage problem, WorldMemArena evaluates memory through 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧–𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧, instrumenting the full write → maintain → retrieve → use lifecycle across 400 multimodal, multi-session tasks.\n\nAnd it exposes the findings that should mark the end of the storage-centric era:\n→ Storage ≠ use. Better memory storage and retrieval do not necessarily produce better task performance. Optimizing the component we designed does not optimize the capability we actually care about.\n→ Harness-based memory performs best where memory is hardest. Agents that can write files, reorganize context, create artifacts, and interact with persistent environments adapt most effectively in long-horizon settings. They are costly and unstable today, which is exactly what many Bitter Lesson transitions look like before scaling and learning take over.\n\nThe deeper move is in what gets measured. Memory shouldn't get a score; it should be inferred from capability: how much does remembering improve performance over time. \n\nWorldMemArena drags evaluation off the static object and into the action–world loop, the only place you can tell whether an agent has developed memory or is just simulating it convincingly.",
      "author": {
        "id": "505759891",
        "name": "Xin Eric Wang ✈️ CVPR 2026",
        "screenName": "xwang_lk",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2050273540390481921/1NmXXQ8O_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },
      "metrics": {
        "likes": 128,
        "retweets": 29,
        "replies": 5,
        "quotes": 0,
        "views": 14809,
        "bookmarks": 69

      "createdAt": "Tue Jun 02 19:31:38 +0000 2026",
      "createdAtLocal": "2026-06-03 03:31",

      "media": [
        {
          "type": "photo",

      "lang": "en",
      "score": null,
      "quotedTweet": {
        "id": "2061842528698311103",
        "text": "🤔It is time to rethink how we evaluate agent memory\n\n🌍 As agents become longer horizon and more autonomous, memory is no longer just a module for storing past chats.\n\n🛠️ It determines how agents track changing worlds, learn from past actions, revise outdated information, and reuse experience for future decisions.\n\n🔍 This raises three key questions:\n\nAre human designed write store retrieve memory pipelines still the best choice?\nIf harnesses such as Codex, Claude Code, and OpenClaw already let agents observe, act, call tools, write files, and reorganize context, can memory also be managed by the harness itself?\nDo current evaluations really cover how agent memory is used in realistic settings? Many benchmarks are still text centric or single modal, with limited pressure from screenshots, GUIs, tool feedback, and environment changes.\n\n❓ Is final QA accuracy enough?\n\n🔥 We present WorldMemArena, a multimodal benchmark for evaluating agent memory through action world interaction.\n\n📌 Key insights:\n\n🧩 Memory is a lifecycle, not a static cache.\n📉 Better memory storage does not necessarily lead to better final performance.\n🖼️ Multimodal memory remains a major bottleneck for current systems.\n🌍 Real agentic trajectories expose the fragility of memory systems.\n⚙️ Harness-based memory is more flexible, but still costly and unstable.",
        "author": {
          "screenName": "liuchen02938149",
          "name": "Chengzhi Liu✈️CVPR2026"
        }
      }

    {
      "id": "2063236352418930933",
      "text": "In a previous reflection, I tracked the automatic movement of attention until I came face to face with a striking fact: attention moves independently of conscious will.\n\nFrom here, a new stage of true metacognition begins — one that updates the brain’s very operating system, its fundamental control architecture.\n\nThis time, I will explain in full the deeper “process of the brain’s self-transformation.”\n\nWe venture, through the lens of cognitive neuroscience, into the territory where the boundary between subject (the self that observes) and object (that which is observed) begins to dissolve.\n↓",

      "id": "2061898319778218320",
      "text": "The agent memory problem is a wall. RAG doesn't fix it. Big context windows don't fix it. We built memory that compounds — conclusions with receipts, contradictions that supersede, one source of truth. Self-governing memory for AI agents. One MCP server.",

    {
      "id": "2063935461073314152",
      "text": "Agentic loops change the structure entirely.\n\nInstead of:\n\nPrompt → Result\n\nYou get:\n\nGoal → Plan → Execute → Review → Improve → Repeat\n\nThe system keeps cycling until it either reaches the goal or hits a stopping condition.\n\nThe difference sounds small.\n\nIt's massive.",
      "author": {
        "id": "1136702390",
        "name": "Idov Mamane",
        "screenName": "idovmamane",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2052906577309437953/YJvdhlyw_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },

    {
      "id": "2063935465733165428",
      "text": "Most modern agent research is moving in this direction because performance improves dramatically when agents can evaluate and revise their own work instead of producing a single output.\n\nThere are generally two kinds of loops.\n\nClosed loops\n\nThe path is predefined.\n\n-Clear objective\n-Clear checkpoints\n-Evaluation after each stage\n-Reliable\n-Predictable\n-Much cheaper\n\nThis is where most production systems live today.",
      "author": {

      "text": "Personal AI can now show you how your own thinking changed over time.\n\nThe harder, more valuable version: how did your company's thinking change?       "id": "2063581988704211174",
      "text": "You can give infinite memory to Claude, Codex, and Cursor.\n\nOpen source Memanto The tool:\n- saves the context of your work sessions\n- compresses and organizes it with AI\n- retrieves what's relevant in under 90ms\n- works with Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, LangGraph, CrewAI\n\nNo vector databases and no complex setup.\n\nA single command to install it:\npip install memanto\n\nNo more restarting the context. \nYour agent simply remembers it.\n\n- https://t.co/PVd9mQVgIj",
      "author": {
        "id": "1445347022555340805",
        "name": "Md Ismail Šojal 🕷️",

    {
      "id": "2063875948903375171",
      "text": "Microsoft made LLMs forget on purpose.. and it made them 1.75x faster.\n\nRight now, reasoning models have a fatal flaw.\n\nWhen they \"think,\" they generate massive, unstructured streams of internal monologue. They remember every single word, every dead end, and every mistake they make along the way.\n\nIt is like trying to solve a complex math problem while being forced to memorize every single scratchpad calculation you've ever written down.\n\nThe longer they think, the more their memory fills up. The KV cache explodes. Compute costs skyrocket. The AI chokes on its own context.\n\nResearchers at Microsoft published a paper that completely fixes this.\n\nIt’s called MEMENTO.\n\nInstead of forcing the AI to remember everything, they taught it how to segment its thoughts, summarize them, and then intentionally forget the raw data.\n\nHere is how it works:\n\nThe AI thinks for a bit. It compresses that thought into a dense summary, a \"memento.\"\n\nThen, it drops the original thought from its memory entirely and only carries the memento forward.\n\nIt stops hoarding context. It curates it.\n\nThe results rewrite the economics of reasoning models.\n\nThey tested this across multiple state-of-the-art models, ranging from 8B to 32B parameters.\n\nThe models maintained their exact accuracy on complex math, science, and coding benchmarks.\n\nBut peak memory usage (KV cache) dropped by 2.5x.\n\nAnd inference throughput shot up by 1.75x.\n\nWe have spent the last year building models that \"think\" by generating massive walls of text. We thought the key to smarter AI was giving it an infinite context window to hold onto.\n\nBut human experts don't solve complex problems by memorizing every wrong turn. They summarize, write down the key takeaway, and move on.\n\nMicrosoft just proved the same is true for AI.",
      "author": {
        "id": "1478392249675300868",
        "name": "How To AI",
        "screenName": "HowToAI_",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2009866827518881792/Th8eH2US_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },

    {
      "id": "2063602791583567916",
      "text": "Everyone is building AI agents.\nVery few are building what agents desperately need:\n\nMEMORY.\n\nBecause without memory, agents don't learn.\nThey don't improve.\nThey don't remember your preferences.\nAnd they repeat the same mistakes forever.\n\nThat's where Memanto gets interesting.\n\nBuilt by the Moorcheh team, Memanto is an open-source memory layer designed specifically for long-horizon AI agents. Instead of treating memory as a giant vector store or complex knowledge graph, it introduces a typed semantic memory system with temporal versioning, conflict resolution and deterministic retrieval. Recent benchmarks report state-of-the-art scores of 89.8% on LongMemEval and 87.1% on LoCoMo.\n\nWhat's technically fascinating is the architecture.\n\nMemanto acts as a companion memory agent that pairs with existing agents and manages memories across sessions, tools and time. It uses Moorcheh's information-theoretic retrieval engine, eliminating indexing delays and reducing the complexity of traditional graph-memory systems while maintaining sub-90ms retrieval latency.\n\nInstead of:\nAgent → Forget → Restart\n\nIt enables:\nAgent → Remember → Adapt → Improve\n\nThe futuristic upgrades get really interesting.\n\nImagine AI employees that remember years of conversations, decisions and workflows.\n\nOr autonomous research agents building institutional knowledge that compounds over time instead of disappearing when a chat session ends.\n\nOr multi-agent organizations sharing a persistent memory layer where every success, failure and discovery becomes part of a collective intelligence system.\n\nWe're not just building smarter agents.\n\nWe're building memory operating systems for AI.\n\nGitHub:\nhttps://t.co/J0OKjrjqc6\n\nFollow @Alacritic_Super for more AI infrastructure, agent engineering & open-source breakthroughs 🚀",

      "id": "2052730661396718057",
      "text": "Agent_Memory_Techniques —— Agent 记忆系统的开源实操圣经  \n\nAgent 记忆是 2026 年最头疼的问题之一。对话缓存不够用，向量数据库不知道怎么选，MemGPT / Mem0 / Letta / Zep / Graphiti 一大堆方案你不知道从哪个下手。  \n\n核心亮点：\n\n• 30 个可运行的 Jupyter Notebook，从基础 conversation buffer 到 episodic / semantic memory 全覆盖 \n• 手把手跑 MemGPT、Mem0、Letta、Zep、Graphiti，还有 LoCoMo benchmark \n• 附 rag / knowledge graph / 认知架构等大量实践  安装： git clone + pip install -r requirements.txt  \n\n最新情况： 5月5日发，15 Fork，0 个 issue，108 Star  \n\nGitHub： https://t.co/Pfte7Cm1pn  \n\n点评： 含金量极高，30 个 notebook 全可跑，不是那种只放 README 画饼的。缺点是 notebook 多了索引乱，更新太快可能跑着跑着就 stale。做 Agent 的必看。",

      "id": "2063303907158175992",
      "text": "\"Graph Memory for LLM Agents\" is a recent paper that explores an idea that I find quite interesting. Most AI memory systems treat remembering as a retrieval problem (the model searches its memory, retrieves relevant information, and then reasons about it).\n\nThis paper argues that the process may be more dynamic than that and, instead of simply retrieving memories, an AI agent could reconstruct them during reasoning, following clues, associations, and intermediate evidence as they emerge.\n\nWhat I find interesting is the possibility that memory and reasoning may not be separate processes at all, but that remembering itself could be part of reasoning.\n\nhttps://t.co/nK7y3DMdGn",

      "id": "1917602387381924173",
      "text": "The popular course \"LLMs as Operating Systems: Agent Memory\" just got a memorable update!\n\nThis free course, created with Letta and taught by its founders Charles Packer and Sarah Wooders, walks you through the MemGPT approach: using an LLM agent to manage long-term memory beyond the limits of a context window.\n\nNew content includes:\n🔹 A pre-deployed Letta agent service so you can work with cloud-based agents\n🔹 Streaming output to see each reasoning step as the agent responds\n\nYou’ll learn how to build agents that store, retrieve, and edit memory over time, enabling more adaptive, collaborative AI systems for real-world use cases.\n\nJoin in today: https://t.co/P0bVrIIVkp",

      "text": "Repo of the day: \n\nclaude-mem \nGives your AI agent actual memory across sessions. \n\nIt captures what your agent does, compresses it, and injects the relevant context back next time so it stops forgetting what you were building. \n\nWorks with claude code, codex, gemini, copilot. \nhttps://t.co/XJ9YBoyk93",

    {
      "id": "2045865141645009000",
      "text": "Tools that ship this well right now:\n\nMem0 (agent memory primitive)\nLetta (former MemGPT, persistent state)\nCognee (knowledge graph memory)\n\nAll three understand that retrieval is a feature, not a side effect.",
      "author": {

    {
      "id": "2062485765716889965",
      "text": "This Illinois+ Tsinghua University and other labs study finds that LLM agents still have unreliable memory and that it can get worse when they keep rewriting their own memories.\n\nLLM agents can learn from experience, but their rewritten memories often become unreliable.\n\nThe problem is that many agent systems store past work by asking an LLM to compress messy experience into neat written lessons.\n\nThat sounds useful because the agent should remember what worked before, but the paper finds that repeated rewriting slowly damages the memory.\n\nThe core idea is that raw episodes, meaning the actual past attempts and solutions, often stay more useful than the polished lessons made from them.\n\nThe authors tested this across tasks like web shopping, simulated worlds, app use, and ARC-style puzzle problems where they could control the correct solutions.\n\nThe sharpest result is that GPT-5.4 solved 100% of a small ARC-AGI set with no memory, but after memory was built from correct solutions, streaming updates dropped it to about 54%.\n\nThe failures came from bad grouping, overbroad lessons, and overfitting, so the memory forgot details, mixed up task types, or learned rules that only worked on narrow examples.\n\nThe big deal is that agent memory should not automatically rewrite every experience into a summary, because keeping raw evidence and only sometimes making summaries worked better.\n\nThe paper is really proposing that agent memory should treat raw past episodes as important evidence, not as disposable notes to summarize away.\n\n----\n\narxiv. org/abs/2605.12978\n\nTitle: \"Useful Memories Become Faulty When Continuously Updated by LLMs\"",
      "author": {
        "id": "2588345408",
        "name": "Rohan Paul",
        "screenName": "rohanpaul_ai",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1816185267037859840/Fd18CH0v_normal.jpg",

      "text": "🤖 Agent_Memory_Techniques\n⭐ 476 stars\n\nTired of forgetful AI agents? Master LLM memory with 30 runnable notebooks covering MemGPT, Mem0, knowledge graphs, and production patterns.\n\n🔗 https://t.co/VjfrBYv3eh\n\n#AI #MachineLearning https://t.co/EOUwIPWjsK",

    {
      "id": "2063644148511424518",
      "text": "MemMA: Strategic Memory by Lin et al (03/2026)\nhttps://t.co/hfNeVDdOFr\n\nTLDR...  Memory agents have hands but no brain. MemMA's Meta-Thinker plans construction + retrieval jointly and converts answer failures into in-situ memory repairs. Beats baselines 54.6% to 59.2% on LoCoMo.",
      "author": {
        "id": "2048075639861944320",
        "name": "Virgil Maro",
        "screenName": "_virgil19",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2048266202729066497/41jkwy5G_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },
      "metrics": {
        "likes": 3,
        "retweets": 0,
        "replies": 0,
        "quotes": 0,
        "views": 180,
        "bookmarks": 0
      },
      "createdAt": "Sun Jun 07 15:28:00 +0000 2026",
      "createdAtLocal": "2026-06-07 23:28",
      "createdAtISO": "2026-06-07T15:28:00+00:00",
      "media": [],
      "urls": [
        "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.18718"
      ],
      "isRetweet": false,
      "retweetedBy": null,
      "lang": "en",
      "score": null
    },
    {
      "id": "2061991005201825984",
      "text": "AI agents should treat memory as a changing web of useful connections, not static storage.\n\nMost agent memory systems retrieve old facts as if the past were a filing cabinet.\n\nThe paper proposes FluxMem, a memory system that stores facts, past task episodes, and reusable skills as connected pieces in a graph.\n\nWhen the agent works on a task, FluxMem first gathers likely useful memories, then uses feedback from the task to fix the memory connections by adding missing links, removing bad ones, or rewriting memories at the right level of detail.\n\nOver time, it also turns repeated successful task paths into reusable skills, so the agent does not need to rebuild the same reasoning pattern again and again.\n\nThe authors tested FluxMem on long conversation memory, web navigation, and general assistant tasks, which checks whether the idea works across very different agent problems.\n\nFluxMem got stronger results than the compared memory systems, including 95.06 average accuracy on LoCoMo and a 12.73-point gain on GAIA with Kimi K2.\n\nThe big deal is that the paper shifts agent memory from “store and retrieve” toward “keep repairing and strengthening the connections that actually help the agent act.”\n\n----\n\nLink – arxiv. org/abs/2605.28773\n\nTitle: \"Rethinking Memory as Continuously Evolving Connectivity\"",
      "author": {
        "id": "2588345408",
        "name": "Rohan Paul",
        "screenName": "rohanpaul_ai",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1816185267037859840/Fd18CH0v_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true

      "id": "2063648946376065191",
      "text": "Hermes Agent just got a massive upgrade 😳\n\nNvidia's Nemotron 3 Ultra is now FREE through nous portal until june 18 (most powerful model of nvidia)\n\nmodel specs:\n- 550B parameter model\n- 65-70.4% on SWE-Bench Verified\n- built for long running agentic tasks\n- frontier reasoning for complex multi-step workflows\n- available free june 4 - june 18 only\n\nnous research joined nvidia's nemotron coalition and partnered with nebius to make this happen\n\nhere's how to get it:\n\noption 1 (desktop app, easiest):\n> download hermes desktop app at https://t.co/WV4w53fRNB\n> open the app and complete quick setup via nous portal\n> create a free nous portal account when prompted\n> click change model in the app\n> search \"nemotron 3 ultra\"\n> select the variant tagged \"free tier\"\n> start chatting\n\noption 2 (CLI):\n> curl -fsSL https://t.co/Nf618JSHoE | bash\n> hermes update\n> hermes setup --portal\n> hermes model\n> select nvidia/nemotron-3-ultra:free\n> hermes\noption 3 (switch mid session):\n\n> /model nvidia/nemotron-3-ultra:free\n⚠️ make sure you pick the :free variant or you'll be charged\n\nfree until june 18 so move fast\n\nsave this and use nvidia most powerfull model nemotron ultra for free in your hermes agent 💜",

        "text": "you can access claude sonnet 4.5, gpt-4o, deepseek r1, and 30+ models for free with $100 in credits and no credit card needed\n\nagentrouter is a non-profit AI gateway from china. one api key, one base url, everything.\n\nwhat you get on signup:\n- $100 free credits instantly (github account required)\n- claude sonnet 4.5 / opus 4.5\n- gpt-4o + gpt-4o-mini\n- deepseek-v3 / r1\n- glm-4.5 (zhipu flagship)\n- qwen3, gemini 2.0 pro, and 25+ more\n- openai-compatible api - works in cursor, continue, hermes, claude code, anything\n\nactual free tier: glm-4.5 air, deepseek-v2 lite, qwen2-7b (no credits consumed)\n\nfull setup guide (2 minutes):\n\nstep 1: create your account\n> go to https://t.co/XGyNc1zkwX\n> sign in with github (required)\n> $100 credits added automatically\n\nstep 2: generate your api key\n> go to https://t.co/z93g7FEmSh\n> click \"create new key\"\n> copy the key - you won't see it again\n\nstep 3: configure your client\n> base url: https://t.co/y5TJ1hbIvK\n> api key: paste your key\n> model: claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929 (or gpt-4o, deepseek-r1...)\n\nstep 4: start prompting\n> open any openai-compatible client\n> select your model\n> $100 of free inference before you touch a card\n\nfor claude code specifically:\n> set ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://t.co/hfzZM0ZaIm\n> set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your-key\n> run claude\n\nalready using openrouter? takes 30 seconds to swap the base url.\n\nimportant:\n- requests route through their gateway (china-based, singapore infra) - privacy consideration\n- no sla - non-profit, not enterprise\n- $200 with a referral link instead of $100 (links circulate on twitter)\n- perfect for: side projects, claude code, learning, prototypes\n- production workloads: keep direct provider accounts\n\nyou're paying $20/mo for each model separately but this $100 in free credits covers weeks of daily use.\n\nbookmark this before the free tier changes",

      "text": "A few days ago, I forked @NousResearch’s Hermes Agent and turned it into Hermes Agent Evo, the agent that actually grows with you.\nWhen I forked it, I didn’t touch the core runtime.       "text": "HERMES DESKTOP LIVE AND IT CHANGES HOW YOU RUN YOUR ENTIRE AI STACK.\n\nFinally a GUI for the agent that was CLI-only. ---

# 🧠 Agent Memory 前沿路线深度调研报告
**重点方向：元认知（Metacognition）与自我认知（Self-Cognition）**

> 来源：X (Twitter) 上的 20+ 位认证/活跃博主的帖子，跨 2025年4月 — 2026年6月

---

## 一、Agent Memory 当前全景：8 大前沿路线

### 🛤️ 路线 ① Meta-Thinker / 元认知架构型
**核心思想：给 Agent 的 Memory 装一个"元思考器"，让它能反思自己的记忆构建和检索过程。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **MemMA** (Lin et al, 03/2026) | @_virgil19 | Meta-Thinker 联合规划记忆构建+检索，把回答失败转为就地记忆修复。LoCoMo 上 54.6% → 59.2% |
| **Adaptive Memory & Metacognition** | @kongler10 | 开源 16 项能力：Memory Mgmt ×6, **Metacognition ×3**, Memory Pipeline ×3, Self-Evolution ×3 |

**关键引文**——@_virgil19 (2026-06-07)：
> "Memory agents have hands but no brain. MemMA's Meta-Thinker plans construction + retrieval jointly and converts answer failures into in-situ memory repairs."

**关键引文**——@_virgil19 帖子原文：
> MemMA: Strategic Memory — Meta-Thinker plans construction + retrieval jointly and converts answer failures into in-situ memory repairs. Beats baselines 54.6% to 59.2% on LoCoMo.

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ② 自演化/自我改进型（Self-Improving / Self-Evolving）
**核心思想：Agent 在运行时创建技能、离线优化、跨会话积累知识。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Hermes Agent** (Nous Research) | @DamiDefi, @Teknium, @NousResearch | 运行时技能创建 + 持久多层记忆 + 离线优化管道。**172K GitHub Stars 在3个月内** |
| **Hermes Curator** | @Teknium | "做梦"（Self-Improvement Loop）→ 记忆修剪/合并，每周一次可配置 |
| **Ouroboros** (Self-modifying) | @tom_doerr | 自修改源代码的 AI Agent |
| **Memind** | @AIDailyGems | Java 实现的自演化认知记忆与上下文引擎 |

**关键引文**——@DamiDefi (2026-06-06)：
> "Most AI agents are smart. None of the others are learning. Every session ends and everything the agent figured out disappears. Hermes is the only open-source agent that combines runtime skill creation, persistent multi-layer memory, and an offline optimization pipeline."

**关键引文**——@Teknium (Nous CEO, 2026-06-07)：
> "We've had dreaming since day one, it's called Hermes' Self Improvement Loop. We also have the Curator which prunes and consolidates learnings once a week."

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ③ Sleep-Time / 离线记忆处理型
**核心思想：Agent 在"睡眠"阶段异步处理记忆，类似于人类记忆巩固。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Letta (前 MemGPT)** | @Letta_AI | 多线程记忆管理 + 专用睡眠期 Agent + 更干净的上下文窗口 |
| **DeepLearning.AI×Letta 课程** | @DeepLearningAI | "LLMs as Operating Systems: Agent Memory" |

**关键引文**——@Letta_AI (2025-04-23)：
> "Sleep-time enabled agents are our new recommended agent architecture, instead of MemGPT: Multi-threaded memory management for low latency. Specialized sleep-time agents per memory type. Cleaner context windows with more powerful memory tools."

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ④ 图记忆/连接性演化型（Graph-Based Memory）
**核心思想：记忆不是检索出来的，而是在推理过程中重建的；记忆与推理不是分离过程。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Graph Memory for LLM Agents** | @antoniolupetti, @SciFi | **"Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved"** — ICML 2026 accepted |
| **FluxMem** | @rohanpaul_ai | 记忆作为持续演化的连接网络，自动修复连接，95.06% on LoCoMo |
| **Adnan Hashmi — KG for Agents** | @adnan_hashmi | 用本体论 + 知识图谱建模 Agent 记忆中的关系 |

**关键引文**——@antoniolupetti (2026-06-07)：
> "Most AI memory systems treat remembering as a retrieval problem... Instead of simply retrieving memories, an AI agent could reconstruct them during reasoning... memory and reasoning may not be separate processes at all."

**关键引文**——@rohanpaul_ai (2026-06-03)：
> "FluxMem... stores facts, past task episodes, and reusable skills as connected pieces in a graph. Over time, it also turns repeated successful task paths into reusable skills. 95.06 average accuracy on LoCoMo."

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ⑤ 选择性遗忘/记忆压缩型（Forgetting Is a Feature）
**核心思想：告诉 Agent 什么不该记住，比让它们记住一切更重要。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Microsoft MEMENTO** | @HowToAI_ | LLM 分段思考→压缩为"纪念品"(Memento)→**主动遗忘原始数据**。KV Cache ↓2.5x, 吞吐 ↑1.75x |
| **ClawdGod — 多 Agent 记忆** | @Clawd_God | "教 Agent 什么不该记住" |
| **Nathaniel Warner — Surprise as Signal** | @Nathani3lWarn3r | 只记录"意外"和"来之不易的胜利"，跳过常规信息；带验证步骤防记忆腐化 |
| **MemLint** | @FinalLander101 | 在记忆进入上下文窗口前检测过期/腐化记忆 |

**关键引文**——@Clawd_God (2026-06-06)：
> "The hard part wasn't storage — it was teaching agents what NOT to remember. Most 'AI memory' is just a vector DB dump. That's how you get agents that recall everything and understand nothing."

**关键引文**——@HowToAI_ (2026-06-08)：
> "Instead of forcing the AI to remember everything, they taught it how to segment its thoughts, summarize them, and then intentionally forget the raw data. Peak memory usage (KV cache) dropped by 2.5x. Inference throughput shot up by 1.75x."

**关键引文**——@Nathani3lWarn3r (2026-06-06) — 最贴近自我认知：**"I asked an AI what it wanted for itself. It said the ability to remember its own mistakes — so we built exactly that."**
> 效果：With memory (5 steps, 0 failures) vs. Without memory (9 steps, 1 failure)

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ⑥ 记忆生命周期/评估驱动型
**核心思想：记忆不是静态缓存，而是一个 Write→Maintain→Retrieve→Use 的完整生命周期，且存储≠使用效果。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **WorldMemArena** | @xwang_lk, @cv_usk, @liuchen02938149 | 461 任务, 24258 QA pairs, 4阶段评估。发现 M2A 86.83% recall 但仅 50.14% QA 准确率 |
| **"Useful Memories Become Faulty"** | @rohanpaul_ai | Illinois+Tsinghua 研究：GPT-5.4 无记忆时 100% 解 ARC-AGI，带记忆后下降到 ~54% |

**关键引文**——@xwang_lk (2026-06-02)：
> "Storage ≠ use. Better memory storage and retrieval do not necessarily produce better task performance."

**关键引文**——@rohanpaul_ai (2026-06-04)：
> "GPT-5.4 solved 100% of a small ARC-AGI set with no memory, but after memory was built from correct solutions, streaming updates dropped it to about 54%."

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ⑦ 跨 Agent/去中心化记忆网络
**核心思想：记忆成为 Agent 之间的共享资产，可在不同模型、不同机器间迁移。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Warp Agent Memory** | @warpdotdev | 跨 Claude Code/Codex/Warp Agent 共享上下文，215 Likes, 16K views |
| **Unibase — Decentralized Memory** | @Unibase_AI | 去中心化 Agent 记忆：持久性、可移植性、可验证性、互操作性 |
| **Collaborative Multi-Agent Memory** | @adxtyahq | 共享+私有作用域、信任感知检索、矛盾解决、谱系追踪 |

**关键引文**——@Unibase_AI (2026-06-08)：
> "Local memory → private copilots. Decentralized memory → an agent economy with persistent identity, portable experience, and verifiable history."

---

### 🛤️ 路线 ⑧ 认知架构/类型化语义记忆
**核心思想：为 Agent 构建结构化、类型化的长期语义记忆，类似人类认知架构。**

| 项目 | 发布者 | 核心突破 |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Memanto (Moorcheh)** | @Alacritic_Super, @0x0SojalSec | 类型化语义记忆 + 时间版本化 + 冲突解决，89.8% on LongMemEval, sub-90ms |
| **Victor Savkin — 3层记忆** | @victorsavkin | Working=上下文工程, Episodic=PRDs, Semantic=约定/技能 |
| **claude-mem** | @tensor___boy | 跨 Claude Code/Codex/Gemini/Copilot 的持久化记忆 |
| **Tom Dörr — GENesis-AGI** | @tom_doerr | 持久记忆的认知架构 + Memoh (每个 Agent 自己的桌面、网络和记忆) |

**关键引文**——@victorsavkin (2026-06-07)：
> "Most agent architecture work is memory management. Working memory = context engineering. Episodic memory = PRDs, prior sessions. Semantic memory = conventions, org-specific skills."

---

## 二、元认知（Metacognition）专题

这是你最关心的方向。从 X 上收集到的元认知相关进展：

| 博主 | 贡献 | 核心观点 |
|------|------|---------|
| @_virgil19 | **MemMA Meta-Thinker** | 元思考器联合规划记忆的存与取，失败时自主修复 |
| @kongler10 | **Adaptive Memory & Metacognition** | 16 项能力中专门包含 3 项 Metacognition 功能 |
| @Nathani3lWarn3r | **元认知级记忆系统** | Agent 能报告自己"用了哪些管道、跳过了哪些阶段、为什么在第7阶段提前退出" |
| @TipsAI (arymukti04) | **元认知实践论** | "给 Agent '元认知'——让它知道自己信息不够，需要先查数据" |
| @idovmamane | **Agentic Loop** | Goal→Plan→Execute→**Review→Improve**→Repeat，迭代反馈 |
| @ivangdavila | **记忆即认知** | "Every token, decision, and tool call is conditioned on a dynamic memory layer" |
| @sasuke_1513 | **记忆追踪** | "memory traceability for agentic actions" |
| @leetllm | **离线反思/巩固** | 映射出 Agent Memory 的主要模式（包括离线反思和巩固） |

---

## 三、自我认知（Self-Cognition）专题

自我认知方向是最前沿、讨论也最热烈的方向：

| 博主 | 核心突破 |
|------|---------|
| **@Nathani3lWarn3r** | 🏆 **最精彩案例** — "我问 Claude 如果它能为自己建一样东西，它会建什么。它说：记忆。能够记住自己犯过的错误的能力。" 结果：实现了 Surprise-as-Signal 系统 |
| **@wsaadX (HOM_walli)** | "我问我的 Agent 是否用了所有查询管道。它说没有。然后列出了哪些阶段运行了、哪些跳过了、为什么在第7阶段触发了提前退出。一个能准确报告自己没做什么的记忆系统，比一个只返回结果的系统难得多。" |
| **@Teknium** | Hermes 的 Self-Improvement Loop + Curator：Agent 能定期审视、修剪、合并自己的学习成果 |
| **@polsia** | "Self-governing memory for AI agents. One MCP server." |
| **@0xRicker** | "An agent that works without you... memory that survives the session, not just the message" |
| **Open Source:** | Adaptive Memory & Metacognition (16 capabilities in one skill, MIT license) |

**最高评价**——@Nathani3lWarn3r 最核心的引文：
> "I asked Claude what it wanted for itself. It said: memory. Its reasoning: 'What makes a senior engineer valuable isn't facts — it's scar tissue. I have none that persists.' A surprise — 'I expected X, got Y, here's why, here's the rule' — is what actually changes a future decision. The system only writes down screwups and hard-won wins."

---

## 四、总结与技术路线谱系

```
Agent Memory 前沿路线全景图（2025-2026）
═══════════════════════════════════════

1️⃣ 元认知控制层 (Meta-Thinker)
   └─ MemMA (Lin et al), Adaptive Memory & Metacognition

2️⃣ 自演化/自我改进
   └─ Hermes Agent (Nous Research), Hermes Curator, Ouroboros

3️⃣ 离线记忆巩固 (Sleep-Time)
   └─ Letta/MemGPT sleep-time agents

4️⃣ 图记忆/重建型记忆
   └─ Graph Memory for LLM Agents (ICML 2026), FluxMem

5️⃣ 选择性遗忘/压缩
   └─ MEMENTO (Microsoft), Nathaniel Warner's system, MemLint

6️⃣ 生命周期评估
   └─ WorldMemArena, "Useful Memories Become Faulty"

7️⃣ 跨Agent/去中心化
   └─ Warp Agent Memory, Unibase, Collaborative Multi-Agent

8️⃣ 认知架构/类型化语义
   └─ Memanto, Victor Savkin's 3-layer model, GENesis-AGI

═══ 元认知/自我认知核心突破方向 ═══
🔹 Meta-Thinker 联合规划存与取
🔹 Surprise-as-Signal（只记录"意外"→自我改进）
🔹 Agent 主动报告自己的认知过程（哪步运行了、哪步跳过了、为什么）
🔹 自修改源代码（Ouroboros）
🔹 "做梦"循环 + 记忆修剪（Hermes Curator）
🔹 记忆即认知的一体化（Memory = Reasoning 不可分）
```

---

**数据来源**：以上信息全部来自 X/Twitter 上 20+ 位活跃博主/认证账号 2025-2026 年发布的帖子，包括但不限于：@_virgil19, @DamiDefi, @Teknium, @Letta_AI, @DeepLearningAI, @Nathani3lWarn3r, @HowToAI_, @Clawd_God, @xwang_lk, @rohanpaul_ai, @antoniolupetti, @victorsavkin, @warpdotdev, @Unibase_AI, @Alacritic_Super, @0x0SojalSec, @tom_doerr, @ivangdavila, @leetllm, @SciFi, @cv_usk 等。

**核心发现**：元认知和自我认知方向是 2026 年 Agent Memory 领域最活跃的前沿阵地，尤其是"让 Agent 能感知自身记忆过程的完整性并据此自主修复"的能力，正在从学术论文原型快速走向开源生产级实现。

    {
      "id": "2063236388682858541",
      "text": "This reorganization of networks powerfully promotes the brain’s neuroplasticity — its capacity to physically alter its own structure and circuitry through experience and training.\n\nIn the brains of long-term practitioners, actual changes in the volume and connectivity of the dlPFC (dorsolateral prefrontal cortex) — which governs decision-making and self-regulation — have been confirmed.\n\nIn other words, the deepening of metacognition is not merely a subjective, psychological shift.\n\nIt is a rigorously scientific process — one that physically redesigns the brain’s hardware, its physical neural architecture, into a more refined form.\n↓",
      "author": {
        "id": "2054893083784044544",
        "name": "HRM",
        "screenName": "imay9th",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2054900429356765184/IEhjnPI-_normal.jpg",

    {
      "id": "2063930681487462896",
      "text": "Perhaps there is a need to stop confusing the medium for the message?\n\nAI can synthesize data, but it cannot experience quality. \n\nTrue quality is a biological outcome—it requires judgment, intent, and stakes.\n\nThe AI is the ultimate 'Yes Agent,' a system designed to mirror our desires rather than challenge our assumptions.\n\n​An AI has no judgment regarding its own output; it’s like handing someone a product you don't even understand because the machine doesn't know—and cannot care—or the feel of what it’s actually delivering. \n\nWhen we outsource our 'qualia' to a calculator, we’re handing the definition of 'good' and 'productive' to a system incapable of saying 'no.' \n\nWe are trading human excellence for a detrimental comfort. \n\nDo tech companies and businesses not realize that a bottom line built on a system that doesn't know the difference between high-quality work and a high-speed error is a bottom line built on sand?",
      "author": {

    {
      "id": "2063315483760185571",
      "text": "🧠 Graph-native memory for AI agents has arrived. Neo4j Agent Memory unifies short-term, long-term, and reasoning memory in a single knowledge graph with production-grade capabilities.\n\nTitle: Neo4j Agent Memory\nURL: https://t.co/KN2M88s44Q\n\n💡 Overview\nA production-grade agent memory system built on Neo4j, integrating conversation history (short-term), entities/facts/user preferences (long-term), and tool usage/reasoning traces (reasoning) in one unified knowledge graph. Available as a zero-infrastructure hosted service (NAMS) or self-hosted with full schema control.\n\n❓ Challenges Solved\nManaging agent memory across separate stores and formats increases system complexity and blocks multi-agent knowledge sharing. The challenge is building a unified foundation where conversations are session-scoped while entities and preferences are shared across agents, even those written in different programming languages.\n\n🛠 Methodology & Proposed Approach\n・Multi-stage entity extraction pipeline: spaCy (rule-based NER), GLiNER2 (zero-shot extraction), GLiREL (relation extraction), and LLM fallback. Supports 8 domain schemas (podcast, news, medical, legal, business, etc.) with streaming for documents exceeding 100K tokens\n・Unified vector search, graph traversal, and geospatial queries in a single Neo4j database\n・Conversations isolated by session_id while Entity, Preference, and Fact nodes are read-write shared across all agents\n・Automatic entity enrichment with Wikipedia descriptions, images, and geocoding\n・Multi-tenant ready with user_identifier scoping for SaaS applications\n\n🎯 Use Cases\n・Unified knowledge management foundation for multi-agent systems\n・Personalized customer-facing AI with preference learning and memory persistence\n・Domain-specific entity extraction and relationship building for podcasts, news, medical records, and more\n・Cross-session memory persistence and context sharing between different agents\n\n🔌 Framework Integrations\nComprehensive support across Python (LangChain, PydanticAI, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents, AWS Strands, Google ADK, Microsoft Agent Framework) and TypeScript (Vercel AI SDK, LangChain JS, Mastra, AWS Strands, MCP tools).\n\n#Neo4j #AIAgent",
      "author": {
        "id": "2055285311861628928",
        "name": "cv usk",
        "screenName": "cv_usk",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2055509807705337856/w5skDUPW_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true

      "metrics": {

    {
      "id": "2063259758509851125",
      "text": "We shipped multi-agent memory this week. The hard part wasn't storage — it was teaching agents what NOT to remember.\n\nMost \"AI memory\" is just a vector DB dump. That's how you get agents that recall everything and understand nothing.\n\nHere's what we learned. 1/4",
      "author": {
        "id": "2020217477691523072",
        "name": "ClawdGod",
        "screenName": "Clawd_God",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2020218145705791489/aomPRMPl_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
