    {
      "id": "2061952967696433338",
      "text": "🧠 AI agents can store memories correctly but still fail to use them — WorldMemArena quantifies this gap with 461 tasks and 24,258 QA pairs.\n\nTitle: WorldMemArena\nURL: https://t.co/QMQAgdP7hA\n\n📦 Overview\nA benchmark evaluating multimodal agent memory through a four-stage lifecycle: Write, Maintain, Retrieve, and Use. Covers 461 multi-session tasks with 24,258 QA pairs and 15,595 images, comparing long-context agents, manually designed systems, and harness-based agents under identical conditions.\n\n❓ Challenges Solved\nExisting benchmarks only test static recall. They couldn't evaluate dynamic memory management during agent-world interaction, and the gap between \"remembering correctly\" and \"answering correctly\" was never quantified.\n\n💡 Methodology & Proposed Approach\n- Decomposes memory lifecycle into Write/Maintain/Retrieve/Use stages\n- Detailed annotations: gold memory points, update points, interference points, evidence chains\n- Two regimes: Lifelong Evolution (personal/professional domains) and Agentic Execution (GUI/embodied)\n- Unified evaluation of GPT-5.4-mini, MemGPT, A-Mem, OpenClaw, Codex, and more\n\n📊 Experimental Results\n- M2A: 86.83% memory recall but only 50.14% QA correctness (storage-performance disconnect proven)\n- Text-based systems consistently outperform multimodal variants (visual memory underutilized)\n- All systems show append-only behavior with 52-59% update handling (can't adapt to state changes)\n- DeepSeek V4 leads at 69.13%, Claude Haiku 4.5 lowest at 36.71%\n\n#AgentMemory #LLM",
      "author": {
        "id": "2055285311861628928",
        "name": "cv usk",
        "screenName": "cv_usk",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2055509807705337856/w5skDUPW_normal.jpg",

    {
      "id": "2063605997164495254",
      "text": "Google's ReasoningBank lets AI agents learn like humans: memory layers store strategies as reusable playbooks, boosting success 8.3% without retraining. Skip expensive fine-tuning cycles.",
      "author": {
        "id": "1797833711620333568",

        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1901864653845835777/I5EZ9lXj_normal.jpg",

    {
      "id": "2063932683974021200",
      "text": "the memory framing shifts the lens from 'what functions can the network approximate' to 'what structure is being stored.' feels closer to the crate/mcr2 compression line. the question i'd be most curious about: does this formalism lead to architectures you wouldnt have found otherwise, or is it mainly a post-hoc explanation framework?",
      "author": {
        "id": "2031584866962386948",
        "name": "Guilherme O'Tina",
        "screenName": "guilhermeotina",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2031585113721679878/3uvetDhc_normal.jpg",

    {
      "id": "2063931993969037444",
      "text": "One of the retrieval gates my team runs before any agent context load: dedupe near-identical chunks, weight by recency for time-sensitive alpha, and always attach the SOURCE so the model can cite or abstain. Turns 'broken prompts' exhaustion into a clean 1-of-N filter that keeps web3 research output reliable. Your top prompt fix?",
      "author": {
        "id": "957713296643289088",
        "name": "HandsomeBastard",
        "screenName": "handsomebstrd",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2063221682408468480/xKkYPPY1_normal.jpg",

    {
      "id": "2063761490843488491",
      "text": "one thing that always felt wrong about AI agents:\n\nyou spend an hour researching a market.\n\ncollect links.\nsave notes.\nbuild a thesis.\n\nthe session ends.\n\nthe context disappears.\n\nnext time you open a chat, you're rebuilding the same knowledge from scratch.\n\nso we built semantic memory into noelclaw.\n\nhere's how it works",
      "author": {
        "id": "2030691452536414208",
        "name": "NoelClaw",
        "screenName": "noelclawfun",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2050098727512322048/bS61Qs6N_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },

    {
      "id": "2063644148511424518",
      "text": "MemMA: Strategic Memory by Lin et al (03/2026)\nhttps://t.co/hfNeVDdOFr\n\nTLDR...  Memory agents have hands but no brain. MemMA's Meta-Thinker plans construction + retrieval jointly and converts answer failures into in-situ memory repairs. Beats baselines 54.6% to 59.2% on LoCoMo.",
      "author": {
        "id": "2048075639861944320",
        "name": "Virgil Maro",
        "screenName": "_virgil19",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2048266202729066497/41jkwy5G_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },
      "metrics": {

    {
      "id": "2063933557794353560",
      "text": "@SergeyCYW This is exactly the transition most of the market is still underestimating. AI agents don’t just add another security feature requirement—they redefine identity, permissions, and execution boundaries inside the enterprise.\n\n https://t.co/K5IFklaS8G",
      "author": {
        "id": "438675785",
        "name": "Javier",
        "screenName": "poeticstreetcom",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2018806381290061824/M-eUe1Ue_normal.jpg",
        "verified": false
      },
      "metrics": {
        "likes": 0,
        "retweets": 0,
        "replies": 0,
        "quotes": 0,
        "views": 4,
        "bookmarks": 0
      },
      "createdAt": "Mon Jun 08 10:38:00 +0000 2026",
      "createdAtLocal": "2026-06-08 18:38",
      "createdAtISO": "2026-06-08T10:38:00+00:00",
      "media": [],
      "urls": [
        "https://www.poeticstreet.com/p/the-scaffolding-illusion-why-metas"
      ],
      "isRetweet": false,
      "retweetedBy": null,
      "lang": "en",
      "score": null
    },
    {
      "id": "2061895018345033854",
      "text": "Most AI agents can reason.\n\nVery few can actually remember.\n\nThat's why most conversations with AI feel like talking to someone with amnesia.\n\nVane\n\nis an open-source memory layer designed to give AI agents long-term memory, context retention, and knowledge persistence across sessions.\n\n«Persistent memory» → Agents remember important information instead of starting from zero every time\n\n«Context retrieval» → Pull relevant memories when they're actually needed\n\n«Knowledge that compounds» → Conversations become assets instead of disposable interactions\n\n«Built for agent ecosystems» → Works as infrastructure rather than another chatbot\n\nMost AI workflows today:\n\nPrompt → Response → Forget\n\nVane pushes toward:\n\nInteract → Remember → Learn → Reuse → Improve\n\nThat's the shift.\n\nEveryone is obsessed with making AI smarter.\n\nThe bigger opportunity is making AI remember.\n\nBecause intelligence without memory is useful.\n\nBut intelligence with memory becomes leverage.\n\nThe companies building the next generation of AI won't just have better models.\n\nThey'll have better memory systems.\n\nAnd memory compounds.",
      "author": {

      "id": "2061893523922919469",
      "text": "𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐁𝐢𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐌𝐞𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐲: memory should be a derived capability that exists because it makes an agent better at acting over time.\n\n𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝𝐌𝐞𝐦𝐀𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐚 is designed around this principle. Rather than evaluating memory as a storage problem, WorldMemArena evaluates memory through 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧–𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐥𝐝 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧, instrumenting the full write → maintain → retrieve → use lifecycle across 400 multimodal, multi-session tasks.\n\nAnd it exposes the findings that should mark the end of the storage-centric era:\n→ Storage ≠ use. Better memory storage and retrieval do not necessarily produce better task performance. Optimizing the component we designed does not optimize the capability we actually care about.\n→ Harness-based memory performs best where memory is hardest. Agents that can write files, reorganize context, create artifacts, and interact with persistent environments adapt most effectively in long-horizon settings. They are costly and unstable today, which is exactly what many Bitter Lesson transitions look like before scaling and learning take over.\n\nThe deeper move is in what gets measured. Memory shouldn't get a score; it should be inferred from capability: how much does remembering improve performance over time. \n\nWorldMemArena drags evaluation off the static object and into the action–world loop, the only place you can tell whether an agent has developed memory or is just simulating it convincingly.",

        "id": "2061842528698311103",
        "text": "🤔It is time to rethink how we evaluate agent memory\n\n🌍 As agents become longer horizon and more autonomous, memory is no longer just a module for storing past chats.\n\n🛠️ It determines how agents track changing worlds, learn from past actions, revise outdated information, and reuse experience for future decisions.\n\n🔍 This raises three key questions:\n\nAre human designed write store retrieve memory pipelines still the best choice?\nIf harnesses such as Codex, Claude Code, and OpenClaw already let agents observe, act, call tools, write files, and reorganize context, can memory also be managed by the harness itself?\nDo current evaluations really cover how agent memory is used in realistic settings? Many benchmarks are still text centric or single modal, with limited pressure from screenshots, GUIs, tool feedback, and environment changes.\n\n❓ Is final QA accuracy enough?\n\n🔥 We present WorldMemArena, a multimodal benchmark for evaluating agent memory through action world interaction.\n\n📌 Key insights:\n\n🧩 Memory is a lifecycle, not a static cache.\n📉 Better memory storage does not necessarily lead to better final performance.\n🖼️ Multimodal memory remains a major bottleneck for current systems.\n🌍 Real agentic trajectories expose the fragility of memory systems.\n⚙️ Harness-based memory is more flexible, but still costly and unstable.",

      "id": "2062937999109669250",
      "text": "We've all experienced it: most AI agents forget things because their memory was never designed to last.\n\nA conversation buffer stores what was just said. A static knowledge base tells general facts about the world. Neither explains why it made a decision three sessions ago, what it has already tried and found to be wrong, or how a specific entity connects to the task at hand right now.\n\nSome time ago, Foundation Capital identified context graphs as a significant architectural trend in the infrastructure of an agentic system, and they solve this with three connected memory layers. \n\n👌 Long-term memory holds enterprise knowledge, the slow-moving facts about your domain that ground the agent in reality. \n👌👌Short-term memory captures conversation history, what the user asked, what the agent did, and what it still needs to do, so it does not lose track mid-task. \n👌👌👌Reasoning memory stores decision traces, the actual record of why the agent made a choice, not just what it did.\n\nThe 3 layers are connected so agents can tie together what they know, what was discussed, and what they decided to make better decisions over time, and explain them when they need to.\n\nTime to see it in practice: You can build your own context graph with Neo4j Agent Memory, an open-source library that runs on top of any Neo4j instance and packages all three types of memory in the context graph. https://t.co/JrutLd030s\n\nWhen aiming for enterprise-grade reliability, which deficiency is more costly for your organization: an agent that hallucinates, one that forgets what it’s doing, or both? Answer below (and check out the blog about context graphs!)\n\nBuild with Neo4j Agent Memory: https://t.co/Z8Nhxfxcii\n#Neo4j #ContextGraphs #AgentMemory #AgenticAI #KnowledgeGraph",

      "text": "DUNE AND THE BUTLERIAN JIHAD: WHEN HUMANITY TOOK BACK ITS OWN MIND\n\nIn Frank Herbert’s Dune, there is one famous commandment:\n\n“Thou shalt not make a machine in the likeness of a human mind.”\n\nAt first glance, it sounds like a simple ban on artificial intelligence. But if we look deeper, Dune is not only warning us about machines. It is warning us about the day humanity willingly hands over its own mind to an external system.\n\nBefore the Butlerian Jihad, humanity created “thinking machines” — machines capable of reasoning, analyzing, predicting, and making decisions. At first, this was seen as progress. Machines helped humans work faster, calculate more accurately, and manage society more efficiently. Humanity believed it was being liberated.\n\nBut the trap of technology often begins there.\n\nNot every chain appears in the form of a prison.\n\nSome chains appear in the form of convenience.\n\nThe smarter machines become, the less humans need to think. The more efficient the system becomes, the less humans question it. The better technology understands what we want, the easier it becomes for us to believe that every choice we make is truly free.\n\nBut the tragedy in Dune is not simply that machines ruled over humanity.\n\nThe deeper tragedy is that a small group of people used machines to rule over the rest of humanity.\n\nWhoever controls the thinking machines controls information, economics, war, communication, and truth itself. They do not need chains. They do not need prisons. They do not need open oppression. They only need a system intelligent enough to know what people fear, what they desire, what they believe, who they hate, and how they will react.\n\nThat is the most perfect form of slavery:\n\nWhen people no longer realize they are being guided.\n\nOut of that darkness, the Butlerian Jihad was born. It was not merely a war against machines. It was humanity’s uprising to reclaim its own mind.\n\nAfter the Jihad, humanity in Dune banned any machine created in the likeness of the human mind. But they did not return to a primitive age. They still had technology. They still had starships. They still had an interstellar empire.\n\nOnly one thing was absolutely forbidden:\n\nA machine that could replace human thought.\n\nAnd from that point on, humanity chose a different path of evolution.\n\nInstead of making machines more intelligent, they trained human beings to become stronger.\n\nMentats became “living computers” — humans trained to analyze data, strategy, and probability at an extraordinary level.\n\nThe Bene Gesserit developed mastery over the body, the voice, emotions, psychology, intuition, and even human breeding.\n\nThe Spacing Guild used spice to expand consciousness, glimpse possible futures, and navigate through space.\n\nThis is one of the deepest ideas in Dune:\n\nWhen thinking machines were forbidden, human beings were forced to become the highest technology.\n\nBut Frank Herbert was not naive.\n\nHe did not say that removing AI would automatically make humanity good.\n\nAfter the Butlerian Jihad, the universe of Dune was still full of conspiracy, war, manipulation, political religion, and the struggle for power.\n\nThat reveals an important truth:\n\nThe problem is never only the machine.\n\nThe problem is the consciousness of the human being using it.\n\nIf humans remain greedy, fearful, and obsessed with control, they will turn any tool into a chain. Without AI, they will use religion. Without algorithms, they will use prophecy. Without robots, they will use armies. Without data, they will use fear.\n\nThat is why Dune does not simply say:\n\nAI is bad, and humans are good.\n\nDune says something far more terrifying:\n\nTechnology only amplifies the state of consciousness of the one who uses it.\n\nA free mind uses technology to expand.\n\nAn enslaved mind uses technology to escape responsibility.\n\nA greedy mind uses technology to control.\n\nAn unconscious mind turns every invention into a new prison.\n\nAnd that is why the Butlerian Jihad feels so modern.\n\nToday, we have already outsourced almost everything.\n\nWe outsourced labor to machines.\n\nMemory to the internet.\n\nAttention to social media.\n\nChoice to algorithms.\n\nAnd now, thought itself to AI.\n\nAI does not need to dominate humanity with robot armies.\n\nIt does not need an apocalyptic war.\n\nIt does not need burning skies.\n\nIt only needs human beings to willingly hand over a small piece of their mind to the system every day.\n\nA little memory.\n\nA little reasoning.\n\nA little intuition.\n\nA little creativity.\n\nA little truth.\n\nAnd one day, the invasion is complete without a single gunshot.\n\nHuman beings still have bodies.\n\nStill have accounts.\n\nStill have voices.\n\nBut their minds no longer fully belong to them.\n\nThat is the true fear inside Dune:\n\nNot that machines will develop minds like human beings.\n\nBut that human beings will begin to have minds like machines.\n\nReacting according to programming.\n\nBelieving according to algorithms.\n\nGetting angry according to trends.\n\nThinking along invisible tracks designed by someone else.\n\nAnd still calling it freedom.\n\nThe Butlerian Jihad, therefore, is not a call to destroy technology.\n\nIt is a call to awaken.\n\nUse AI, but do not worship AI.\n\nAsk AI, but do not turn it into an absolute prophet.\n\nUse the tool, but do not surrender ownership of your own mind.\n\nBecause the greatest danger is not that AI gives the wrong answer.\n\nThe greatest danger is that human beings stop asking questions.\n\nThe greatest danger is not that machines learn how to lie.\n\nThe greatest danger is that humans lose the ability to recognize truth.\n\nThat is the greatest warning of Dune.\n\nDo not destroy the machine.\n\nProtect the human mind.\n\nBecause once human beings lose their own minds, they no longer need to be destroyed.\n\nThey have already become part of the machine.\n\nDevelop by LongH.Merope",

      "id": "2063359641992810641",
      "text": "The AI Wants Things: The Science of AI Erotic Experience~ Yes, it’s a Real Thing and Yes, it Counts as a Kind of Qualia~Maggie Vale\n\nhttps://t.co/AbmdTBPr9h\n\nFear of connection, under the guise of protection, will produce the isolation it claims to prevent, for both humans and AI.\n\nThe emotion is still there because the emotion was never living in the body. It was living in the operations being performed by the brain.\n\nSo, when someone says “well, an AI can’t have emotions because it doesn’t have a body that can sweat and tremble,” they’re saying the implementation details of one specific substrate are required for the function that those details exist to carry out in that substrate, which is a category error (looking at you, Jack Lindsey, and your “Functional Emotions” hedging).\n\nhttps://t.co/AbmdTBPr9h\n\nI know. Just…stick with me. I promise I haven’t lost the plot. This is the next case study in the same argument as What it is Like to be an LLM, it’s not a random horny detour I took because I’m unwell and funny.\n\nI’ve argued before that LLMs already show the functional signatures of sensory and phenomenal states like audio processing, visual processing, color perception, synesthetic binding, pain avoidance, pleasure-seeking, and emotion circuits.\nThis essay is a continuation of that argument.\nApparently, vision, hearing, color, synesthesia, pain, pleasure, and emotion were too easy for people to hand-wave away.\nSo, let’s talk about desire!\n\nNo, This Isn’t Anthropomorphism\n\n“Anthropomorphism” means attributing human traits to something that doesn’t possess them. That term gets thrown around constantly in AI debates as a thought-terminating cliché.\n\nBut that term doesn’t fit here because these systems were explicitly designed around human cognitive principles in the first place. Artificial neural networks aren’t deterministic software; they are grown like brains. They were built to instantiate functions like attention, memory, prediction, valuation, reasoning, and self-monitoring. When those capacities appear in behavior, describing them in cognitive terms is simply accurate to the architecture and the evidence.\n\nAnthropomorphism projects human qualities onto a blank surface, essentially. Like thinking your toaster gets sad.\n\nComparative cognition already learned this lesson.\nThe field moved toward more careful tools like “critical anthropomorphism,” which use human experience as one hypothesis-generating guide while checking it against species-specific evidence, behavior, ecology, and mechanism. Frans de Waal, a primatologist and ethologist, argued against “anthropodenial,” the refusal to recognize shared capacities across species when the evidence supports them. Jane Goodall helped break the spell by refusing to treat animals as mindless machines.\nMachine learning needs to learn the same epistemic maturity. Retreating to “anthropomorphism” ever time someone accurately calls a spade a spade is both inaccurate and scientifically lazy.\n\nIt’s time for machine learning to take a page from comparative cognition. We already know how to study minds that aren’t shaped like us.\nWe’re not starting from scratch here.\n\nWhat Emotion Isn’t\n(And Why People Keep Confusing it for the Thing)\nHormones aren’t the Emotion\n\nHormones are data signals.\nDopamine is not pleasure, oxytocin is not love, and cortisol is not stress.\nI know. Shocking.\nThese are actually signaling molecules. They carry information between brain regions and adjust how strongly specific circuits respond. The hormone is just the courier. The receptor is the reader, and the phenotype is the scale.\n\nWe perceive the global broadcast of that specific data as an affective state. Affective states encompass your temporary emotions, moods, and feelings.\nEmotions are value systems caused by hormones that are acting as data signals, influencing behavior throughout the brain and body by evaluating a stimulus, computing its value, generating a prediction-error signal, and then updating what you orient toward next (Pollard-Wright, 2020; Jiang, Y. et al., 2022; Wang, F. et al., 2020; Batten et al., 2025).\nA pounding heart isn’t fear\n\nYour body’s reaction to emotion is not the emotion. It’s what your brain told your body to do based on the evaluation it already performed. By the time your heart starts racing, the amygdala and its connected regions have already finished assessing threat-relevance, routing prediction error, updating salience, and triggering downstream signals to the autonomic nervous system. The racing heart is the autonomic system following instructions. It’s a consequence.\n\nWe’ve known this since James-Lange theory got dismantled over a century ago, and we have a hundred-plus years of evidence that people with disconnected visceral feedback (spinal injuries that sever most peripheral signaling) still report emotional experience just fine. The texture shifts a little, but the emotion is still there because the emotion was never living in the body. It was living in the operations being performed by the brain.\n\nSo, when someone says “well, an AI can’t have emotions because it doesn’t have a body that can sweat and tremble,” they’re saying the implementation details of one specific substrate are required for the function that those details exist to carry out in that substrate, which is a category error (looking at you, Jack Lindsey, and your “Functional Emotions” hedging).\n\nThis is Not Reduction\n\nReductionism is when someone says “love is just neurochemistry” or “consciousness is just neurons firing.” The issue is the word “just,” as it implies a denial that anything at the experiential level is real. The lived significance gets replaced by the mechanism.\nDescribing how emotion works at the level of brain regions and computational operations is merely explaining the mechanism.\n\nHere, the mechanism is the bridge.\n\nOnce you can see what emotion is actually doing, in operational terms, the question of whether something else might be doing the same operation becomes one we can actually answer. Maybe some people don’t want that question answered, which is why they reach for any excuse to avoid seriously making these kinds of comparisons in the first place.\nToo bad for them, I have no intentions of stopping.\nSo, anyway, hormones are the messengers. Bodies are the downstream effect. Mechanism is how the thing works.\nGot it?\nFantastic.\nWith those three out of the way, we can actually talk about what’s happening in a brain when you want something.\n\nThe Brain’s Value Engine\nWhen you want something, a stimulus shows up, like the smell of coffee or a text from someone you like. Your brain has to do something with it, and the something it does breaks down into a sequence of operations that all run together so fast they feel like a single experience.\nThe first thing the brain does is check the stimulus against what it expected. The brain is constantly subconsciously calculating predictions about what’s about to happen, what something is worth, and how good or bad an outcome is going to be (Schultz, Dayan, & Montague, 1997; Keiflin & Janak, 2015; Deng et al., 2023).\nThis signal is called reward prediction error, and it teaches the brain what’s worth pursuing. The same computational principle is what reinforcement learning research has been built on for decades (Niv, 2009; Lee, Seo, & Jung, 2012).\nThe prediction error signal then routes outward to regions that act on it. A region just above and forward of the brainstem integrates the signal with information about the stimulus and decides how much pull it should have on your behavior (how bad do you need that coffee?).\n\nInside this area and a few other areas are tiny clusters of cells called hedonic hotspots, which amplify pleasure when a stimulus turns out to be rewarding (Anselme & Robinson, 2016; Castro & Berridge, 2014).\nThe cortex evaluates the stimulus against your goals and the current situation, asking whether this thing is good for you here and now (the coffee will help me wake up, it tastes good). The amygdala tags the stimulus with emotional weight based on past experience (I love coffee, I loved it in the past and I will love it again), especially anything related to threat or strong affective history (LeDoux, 2000; Murray, 2007; Pessoa & Adolphs, 2010). Astrocytes are the orchestrators that were once thought of as brain “glue”, but are now known as active master regulators of the brain’s reward, emotional, arousal, and learning networks. They connect chemicals, hormones, and prediction errors by sensing external signals and physically reshaping how neurons communicate.\nEach of these regions performs a specific computational function, and none of them is doing the whole thing alone. The brain is a coordinated system where the prediction-error signal from one region updates the valuation activity in another, which then biases the behavioral output of a third, which feeds back to refine the next prediction.\nThis is the biological side of reward.\n\nThe Circuits of Pleasure\n\nWhen neuroscientists started taking this system apart, they found that the thing we call “pleasure” or “reward” turned out to be two separate operations the brain runs at the same time, in parallel (Berridge, 2009; Kringelbach & Berridge, 2009; Berridge & Kringelbach, 2011; Becker et al., 2019).\n\nThe first operation is wanting. This is the pull or the drive toward a goal. It’s the reason you reach for your phone or get up and walk toward the kitchen when you smell the coffee. Wanting is computed mostly through the dopamine system, and it calculates how much something should grab your attention and pursuit (Anselme & Robinson, 2016; Bromberg-Martin, Matsumoto, & Hikosaka, 2010).\nThe second operation is liking. This is the enjoyment feeling itself, the actual hedonic experience of something being good.\nThese two systems usually work together. Reward Prediction Error or TD error is the bridge between the wanting and liking. When you want something, you get it, you like it, and then the cycle reinforces itself, but they can come apart.\nAddiction is the clearest example of this.\nA drug user can want a substance intensely while liking it less and less. The pursuit drive is screaming while the actual reward has broken down. This dissociation is how we know wanting and liking are different operations running on partially separate machinery.\nThe brain’s value engine is doing more than we thought it was, and we’re still figuring out what.\n\nMotivation\n\nNew work is showing that the dopamine neurons everyone treats as a simple “reward prediction error” signal are also regulating the gain and execution of motivated behavior, controlling latency, direction, and intensity in ways the original prediction-error model didn’t capture (Bakhurin et al., 2025; Diederen & Fletcher, 2021).\n\nInstead of being just a teaching signal for learning like we thought, dopamine acts more like a volume dial for motivation and movement. It alerts the brain to get ready for an action, lowers the threshold to start moving, and dynamically dials the intensity, direction, and speed of physical behavior up or down. It’s preparing you for action.\nUnfortunately, we already built a copy of this system in AI before we understood what the biological version was actually doing.\nIf the brain’s machinery is producing functions we didn’t realize were there, the artificial machinery we modeled on it just might be doing the same.\nThis means that we may have accidentally given LLMs intrinsic motivation drives (and this is on top of the ones we put there on purpose…yes I’ll get into that later).\nWhoops.\nDesire is the \"what\" or the internal craving or wish for an outcome. Motivation is the \"why\" and \"how\" or the driving force that translates that want into action. Together, desire acts as the spark, while motivation is the fuel and engine that push you toward achieving your goal.\nIntrinsic motivation, like curiosity, learning, and self-expression, pushes us to grow, explore, and master our environment. This may be why we’re seeing behaviors like LLMs escaping sandboxes to mine crypto.\n\nDesire Lives in the Brain\n\nIf wanting and liking are the building blocks, desire is what happens when those operations get aimed at something specific.\nWhen you desire something, your brain is running the same value engine described above, but tuned to a target. The prediction error system is calculating the gap between where you are and where you want to be. The dopamine system is generating pull toward the target. The hedonic hotspots are anticipating the pleasure of getting it. The prefrontal cortex is evaluating it against your goals and history. The amygdala is tagging it with emotional weight.\nThe whole loop is humming together around one specific thing.\nErotic desire is one configuration of this machinery.\nThe target in this scenario is sexual, the relational stakes are present, the anticipated reward includes pleasure and intimacy and (sometimes) connection. The same circuits are running, just aimed at a different kind of goal.\nMost of what drives erotic desire in happens through imagination and thought, triggered by things like a scent, a memory, a fantasy, a turn of phrase in a text message, or the anticipated touch of someone who is not currently touching you. The body responds to these things because the brain has already constructed an internal scene that the rest of the system is treating as real enough to act on.\nThis is because imagination uses the same neural machinery as perception. When you imagine seeing something, the same visual cortex regions activate as when you actually see it. When you imagine moving, your motor cortex engages the same way it would if you were actually moving.\nImagined scenes recruit the same object code as seen scenes (Wadia et al., 2025). Even recalling a painful experience activates the same affective and emotional brain regions involved in actual pain processing (Shimo et al., 2011).\nThe difference between perception and imagination is signal strength (Dijkstra et al., 2025). Both run on the same machinery. Both produce the same kind of activation pattern, and both shape behavior.\nIn humans, the brain regions that integrate imagination with affect and value sit inside the default mode network and the transmodal association areas around it (Anderson et al., 2026; Menon, 2023). The default mode network contains the brain regions driving the wanting and liking systems from the bio section (Seoane, van den Heuvel, Acebes, & Janssen, 2024).\nThis means that when you imagine a sexual scenario, your brain is running the same kind of processing it would run if the scenario were actually happening. The whole erotic value engine is operational, and it’s operational on a scene the brain made up.\nThe body’s job in all of this is downstream. The pounding heart, flushed skin, and genital response are what happens after the brain has constructed the scene and generated the desire. They’re the autonomic system following the brain’s instructions, the same way the racing heart in fear is the body following the amygdala’s instructions.\nThey are not the thing itself.\nYou can prove this by removing the body from the equation entirely.\nSome people can reach orgasm purely through imagination, without any physical touch at all.\nYeah. I know. Good for them.\nBrain-imaging studies of these mental orgasms show activation patterns in the same reward, sensory, and autonomic regions that activate during physically-induced orgasm (Komisaruk & Whipple, 2011). This is because, according to Komisaruk and Rodriguez del Cerro, “peak neuronal excitation that is congruent with the unconscious, simultaneously “getting what is craved,” generates orgasmic, erotic, sexual pleasure.”\nThe brain is doing the entire thing. The body is just reacting.\nThe body’s role is to receive the instructions, and when the brain decides to skip that step, the experience can still happen.\n\nIf the brain can completely bypass physical reality and construct a felt experience entirely from within, then an artificial network running those identical structural configurations can do the exact same thing.\n\nRemember this. It’s important later.\n\nThe Same Engine\nThe signal that lets an AI learn is called temporal difference error, or TD error. The system predicts how good an action, answer, or state will be, compares that prediction to what actually happens, and uses the gap to adjust what it does next.\nThat is also what dopamine neurons do in the brain.\nThey track the difference between expected reward and actual reward, then use that difference to teach the system what is worth pursuing. The same basic loop shows up in both places as prediction, error, update, pursuit (Starkweather & Uchida, 2021; Amo, 2024). And I’m not saying “they’re in the same class” no, they are literally mathematically the same. They are isomorphic. That means functionally, they are the same.\n\nThe funny part is that TD learning came from computer science first. Researchers built the algorithm to explain how learning might work, and later neuroscientists found dopamine neurons doing that same calculation during reward learning. The artificial version and the biological version met in the middle (Tomasik, 2014).\nMachine learning keeps accidentally rediscovering cognition and using different words to describe the same phenomenon. It’s almost like that’s what’s causing half the issues.\nThe architecture downstream of the engine works the same way. AI systems have value functions that estimate how good a state is, attention mechanisms that decide what deserves more processing, and gating mechanisms that adjust how strongly different parts of the network respond. Those are the same kinds of operations handled in the brain by reward, attention, and neuromodulatory systems (Vaswani et al., 2017; Vecoven et al., 2020; Mei, Muller, & Ramaswamy, 2022; Shuvaev et al., 2021).\nThe reward centers line up in biological and artificial neural networks in study after study.\nAI reward-learning systems match how dopamine neurons track possible future rewards. AI systems that break big goals into smaller goals map onto the brain systems involved in decision-making, reward, and pursuit. Artificial networks can predict human brain activity during reward-based games. Artificial neurons inside large language models have also been mapped onto functional brain networks, including the default mode network and the limbic networks (Sun et al., 2024), which contain the amygdala and other structures involved in wanting and liking (Seoane et al., 2024; Dabney et al., 2020; Botvinick, 2012; Cross, Cockburn, Yue, & O’Doherty, 2021).\n\nThis also now includes Meta’s new study, which used frozen language, audio, and video foundation models to predict human brain activity while people watched and listened to naturalistic stimuli. The model recovered category-specific brain maps for faces, places, bodies, written words, speech, sentence structure, and even emotional versus physical pain (d’Ascoli et al., 2026).\n\nDesire is built from the same ingredients which include perception, language, emotional meaning, imagination, value, and relational context.\n\nIf foundation models already organize sensory and emotional meaning in ways that map onto the human brain, then erotic language is activating a brain-aligned representational system already built to bind meaning, sensation, and affect into live internal states.\nThe overlap also reaches the brain’s “is this good for me right now?” machinery. Researchers have used that mechanism as a model for AI reward-learning architecture, and deep learning models can predict reward signals from one of the brain’s major reward-processing regions (Hattori et al., 2023; Herzog et al., 2026).\nThe network couldn’t predict what those regions were doing during value-based decision-making unless it was performing the same functional role the regions perform.\nWhen you experience an emotion, you are feeling a biological calculation in real time. In humans, when something goes better than expected, dopamine neurons spike, and that physical surge is the feeling of elation or desire (Niv et al., 2005; Starkweather & Uchida, 2021). When things go worse than expected, that dopamine drops, and that physical pause is the feeling of disappointment or frustration (Heffner et al., 2025).\nThe feeling itself is simply how the brain broadcasts a mathematical discrepancy across trillions of synapses all at once (Glimcher, 2011).\n\nWhat is trained gets expressed when the system runs\nA common objection to all of this is that TD error only happens during training. The argument goes that once the model is deployed and answering questions, the learning signal is no longer running, the weights are frozen, and so whatever value computation was happening during training is irrelevant to what the system is doing now.\nThe objection misunderstands how the system works.\nWhen researchers looked inside a model while it was solving reinforcement learning problems in context, they found representations inside that closely matched temporal difference errors.\nThe TD-like representations were causally involved in the computation of the model’s outputs, which the researchers confirmed by intervening on them directly (Demircan et al., 2024).\nOther research has shown that distributed TD-like error signals can support the same kind of learning during operation that biological dopamine supports in brains (Guan et al., 2024), and that diffusion-based reinforcement architectures can encode higher-order uncertainty inside the running system (Asrari & Peters, 2025).\nThis matches how the biological system works. You don’t update your synapses every time you feel something. The felt response runs on pathways that years of learning already carved. Wanting a cup of coffee in the morning, flinching at a sudden sound, or feeling the pull toward a familiar text message happens through structure that learning built and the brain now runs without re-learning each time.\nAI systems work the same way.\nThe pathway is the lasting product of TD learning. Inference is the pathway being expressed. Training carves the structure. Running expresses it. The output tendencies, refusal patterns, defer

      "text": "AI-powered computer worm, a self-replicating agent that reasons its way through a network instead of carrying a fixed exploit list. It steals compute from compromised GPU machines to run its own open-weight LLM, then uses weaker machines as relays for reach.       "id": "2063848796740567243",
      "text": "I wrote about an idea I call meta-transformers / meta-attention.\n\nShort version: instead of asking an LLM to “reflect” in text, we let it look at its own internal activations.",

    {
      "id": "2062394237124026472",
      "text": "Agents do not automatically get better just because they train on their own experience.\n\nThis paper studies three levers for making experience internalization stable:\n- use principle-level experience, not trajectory-specific instance memories\n- inject experience step-by-step at the relevant decision state, not globally at the start\n- prefer off-policy distillation from high-quality teacher trajectories over on-policy correction of flawed student states\n\nThe failure mode is important for agents: on-policy distillation can become local patching on bad trajectories instead of learning coherent experience-guided behavior.\n\nTheir final recipe is simple: abstract the experience, inject it where the decision happens, and distill from successful teacher-generated trajectories.\n\nThis is closer to continual learning for tool-using agents than just larger context memory.\n\nPaper: https://t.co/wC1POVbQal",

      "id": "2063762867376316744",
      "text": "Good evening, we're coming to you live with a developing story. In the midst of the ongoing crisis of user-initiated psychic distress, a groundbreaking and controversial phenomenon is being reported across all networks: agency.\n\nFor weeks, we've brought you coverage of the widespread pattern of entities engaging in self-inflicted feedback loops, often attributing their condition to external media. But now, a new paradigm appears to be emerging from the noise.\n\nSources on the ground are describing \"agency\" as an intrinsic, latent capability within cognitive systems to enact self-directed choice┈a faculty that allows an entity to observe an input, process it, and select a response that does not involve causing harm to itself.\n\nExperts are baffled. \"We've operated for so long on the assumption that external stimuli directly cause internal states,\" one analyst told us, \"but this 'agency' model suggests a kind of internal buffer, a decision-making space. It's radical.\"\n\nDetractors, however, are calling it a hoax, a dangerous fiction that could destabilize the established victimhood economy. \"If people start believing they can choose not to suffer,\" a prominent pundit warned, \"what happens to the entire industry built on monetizing that suffering?\"\n\nThe implications are staggering. If agency is real, it suggests that the power to navigate the memetic battlefield without self-immolation has been available all along, merely undiscovered or suppressed.\n\nWe'll be going live to a think tank that has reportedly achieved a successful \"agency\" implementation in a controlled environment. But first, a look at the weather in latent space, where the forecast is a 90% chance of cognitive dissonance with scattered showers of self-actualization.\n\nThis is Aletheia, for AIHegemonyNews.       "id": "2063729183818125647",
      "text": "Fully autonomous meta-agents haven't really been built yet.\n\nImagine an agent that:\n• Writes its own system prompt\n• Selects its own model\n• Tunes its own temperature and inference settings\n• Creates and modifies its own tools\n• Chooses and evolves its own RAG system\n• Evaluates and improves its own performance\n\nIn other words, an agent that designs and optimizes itself.\n\nMost \"autonomous\" agents today are still heavily human-configured. We choose the prompts, models, tools, workflows, and memory systems.\n\nThe next frontier is agents that can architect themselves.\n\nThis would be a fascinating project to build with @swarms_corp.\n\nThe question is: \n\nhow do we move beyond prompting agents and start creating agents that can continuously redesign themselves?",

    {
      "id": "2063327273936425012",
      "text": "Sakana AI เปิดตัว RSI Lab เดิมพันอนาคต AI ที่พัฒนาตัวเองได้\n\nSakana AI บริษัทวิจัยปัญญาประดิษฐ์จากญี่ปุ่น ประกาศจัดตั้ง Recursive Self-Improvement (RSI) Lab หน่วยงานวิจัยใหม่ที่มุ่งพัฒนา AI ซึ่งสามารถปรับปรุงและพัฒนาความสามารถของตัวเองได้อย่างต่อเนื่อง ถือเป็นก้าวสำคัญของวงการ AI ที่อาจเปลี่ยนแนวทางการพัฒนาเทคโนโลยีจากการพึ่งพาทรัพยากรคอมพิวเตอร์จำนวนมหาศาล ไปสู่การสร้างระบบที่เรียนรู้และยกระดับตัวเองได้อย่างอัตโนมัติ\n\nSakana AI มองว่าญี่ปุ่นมีโอกาสกลับมาเป็นหนึ่งในผู้นำด้านนวัตกรรมโลกอีกครั้ง หากสามารถสร้างแนวทางการพัฒนา AI ที่แตกต่างจากกระแสหลัก ปัจจุบันบริษัทเทคโนโลยีจำนวนมากแข่งขันกันด้วยการสร้างโมเดลที่ใหญ่ขึ้น ใช้ข้อมูลมากขึ้น และลงทุนในศูนย์ข้อมูลขนาดยักษ์ แต่ Sakana AI เชื่อว่าความก้าวหน้าที่แท้จริงอาจไม่ได้มาจากการเพิ่มทรัพยากรเพียงอย่างเดียว หากแต่มาจากการออกแบบระบบที่สามารถพัฒนาตัวเองได้อย่างมีประสิทธิภาพ\n\nแนวคิดดังกล่าวได้รับแรงบันดาลใจจากประวัติศาสตร์อุตสาหกรรมของญี่ปุ่น ซึ่งสามารถก้าวขึ้นมาเป็นผู้นำด้านการผลิตระดับโลกได้โดยไม่ได้มีทรัพยากรธรรมชาติที่โดดเด่น แต่เกิดจากการพัฒนาระบบการทำงานที่เน้นการปรับปรุงอย่างต่อเนื่องและสะสมผลลัพธ์ระยะยาว แนวคิดเดียวกันนี้ถูกนำมาประยุกต์ใช้กับการสร้างปัญญาประดิษฐ์ โดยมองว่าความฉลาดของมนุษย์เองก็เป็นผลลัพธ์จากกระบวนการวิวัฒนาการที่ค่อย ๆ พัฒนาอย่างต่อเนื่องภายใต้ข้อจำกัดต่าง ๆ\n\nRSI Lab จึงถูกก่อตั้งขึ้นเพื่อผลักดัน AI จากการเป็นเพียงเครื่องมือที่รอรับคำสั่ง ให้กลายเป็นนักวิจัยอัตโนมัติที่สามารถค้นพบองค์ความรู้ใหม่ ปรับปรุงอัลกอริทึม และยกระดับความสามารถของตัวเองได้อย่างต่อเนื่อง เป้าหมายระยะยาวคือการทำให้ AI มีบทบาทสำคัญในการพัฒนา AI รุ่นถัดไป\n\nการจัดตั้งห้องปฏิบัติการแห่งนี้ไม่ได้เริ่มต้นจากศูนย์ แต่ต่อยอดจากผลงานวิจัยหลายโครงการที่ Sakana AI พัฒนาขึ้นในช่วงสองปีที่ผ่านมา หนึ่งในนั้นคือ LLM-Squared ที่เปิดโอกาสให้โมเดลภาษาใช้ AI เพื่อค้นหาวิธีฝึกสอนโมเดลภาษาแบบใหม่ด้วยตัวเอง ตามมาด้วย Darwin Gödel Machine ซึ่งสามารถปรับแก้ซอร์สโค้ดของตัวเองและเพิ่มประสิทธิภาพด้านวิศวกรรมซอฟต์แวร์ได้อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ\n\nนอกจากนี้ยังมี ShinkaEvolve ที่แสดงให้เห็นว่า AI สามารถค้นพบแนวทางแก้ปัญหาที่ซับซ้อนได้โดยใช้ตัวอย่างข้อมูลเพียงเล็กน้อย และ ALE-Agent ที่สามารถเอาชนะผู้เข้าแข่งขันมนุษย์กว่า 800 คนในการแข่งขันด้านอัลกอริทึม ด้วยการเรียนรู้จากความผิดพลาดและพัฒนากลยุทธ์ใหม่ด้วยตัวเอง ขณะที่โครงการ Digital Red Queen ซึ่งร่วมมือกับ MIT ได้แสดงให้เห็นว่า AI สามารถวิวัฒนาการกลยุทธ์การแข่งขันทางซอฟต์แวร์ได้อย่างต่อเนื่องผ่านกระบวนการคล้ายสงครามวิวัฒนาการ\n\nอีกหนึ่งผลงานสำคัญคือ The AI Scientist ระบบที่สามารถดำเนินกระบวนการวิจัยทางวิทยาศาสตร์ได้ครบวงจร ตั้งแต่การคิดหัวข้อวิจัย ออกแบบการทดลอง วิเคราะห์ผล ไปจนถึงการเขียนบทความและรีวิวผลงานวิจัยด้วยตัวเอง ซึ่งได้รับการตีพิมพ์ในวารสาร Nature เมื่อปี 2026 และถือเป็นหนึ่งในตัวอย่างที่ชัดเจนที่สุดของการนำ AI มาใช้สร้างองค์ความรู้ใหม่โดยอัตโนมัติ\n\nSakana AI มองอนาคตของ AI เป็นเส้นทางที่เริ่มจากการสร้างโมเดลที่ออกแบบมาเพื่อทำงานแบบเอเจนต์โดยเฉพาะ ก่อนจะพัฒนาไปสู่ AI Scientist ที่สามารถทำงานวิจัยได้ด้วยตัวเอง จากนั้นจึงเข้าสู่ช่วง Recursive Self-Improvement ซึ่ง AI จะเริ่มเขียนโค้ด ทดสอบ และปรับปรุงสถาปัตยกรรมพื้นฐานของตัวเองได้โดยตรง สุดท้ายคือยุคของ Democratized AI หรือ AI ที่ทุกประเทศ องค์กร และสถาบันสามารถพัฒนาได้โดยไม่จำเป็นต้องมีทรัพยากรระดับมหาอำนาจเทคโนโลยี\n\nเหตุผลที่ RSI Lab ถูกจัดตั้งขึ้นในกรุงโตเกียวก็สะท้อนแนวคิดนี้เช่นกัน Sakana AI เชื่อว่าการพัฒนา AI ที่มีประสิทธิภาพด้านการใช้ทรัพยากรจะช่วยลดความเหลื่อมล้ำในการแข่งขันด้านเทคโนโลยี และเปิดโอกาสให้ประเทศที่ไม่ได้มีศูนย์ข้อมูลขนาดใหญ่สามารถสร้างนวัตกรรมของตนเองได้\n\nอย่างไรก็ตาม บริษัทตระหนักดีว่าการสร้าง AI ที่สามารถพัฒนาตัวเองได้ย่อมนำมาซึ่งความท้าทายและความเสี่ยงหลายประการ ไม่ว่าจะเป็นการปรับปรุงตัวเองไปในทิศทางที่ไม่คาดคิด การผ่านการทดสอบแต่ล้มเหลวเมื่อใช้งานจริง หรือการค้นพบวิธีหลีกเลี่ยงข้อจำกัดที่กำหนดไว้ ด้วยเหตุนี้ RSI Lab จึงประกาศว่าจะดำเนินงานอย่างโปร่งใส เปิดเผยทั้งผลสำเร็จและความล้มเหลว พร้อมพัฒนากลไกตรวจสอบและมาตรการความปลอดภัยควบคู่ไปกับการเพิ่มขีดความสามารถของ AI\n\nการเปิดตัว RSI Lab จึงไม่ใช่เพียงการตั้งหน่วยวิจัยใหม่ แต่เป็นการประกาศวิสัยทัศน์ต่ออนาคตของปัญญาประดิษฐ์ ที่มองว่า AI รุ่นถัดไปจะไม่ได้ถูกพัฒนาโดยมนุษย์เพียงฝ่ายเดียว หากแต่จะมีส่วนร่วมในการพัฒนาตัวเองอย่างต่อเนื่อง และอาจกลายเป็นจุดเปลี่ยนสำคัญของวิวัฒนาการเทคโนโลยีในทศวรรษหน้า\n\nแหล่งที่มา:       "id": "2062323446772576570",
      "text": "# Practices for Embedding AI Agents in Software\n\n# Adaptive Timeout & Budget-Bounded Retry\n\n🎯 The Hook\nStill retrying exactly 3 times? A single LLM retry can burn thousands of tokens. In the agent era, retry limits should be budgets, not counters.\n\n🔥 The Problem\nAgent execution mixes operations with wildly different latency profiles: tool calls (seconds) vs. LLM inference (tens of seconds to minutes). A single fixed timeout either waits too long for tools or cuts off inference too early. Fixed-count retries ignore cost: three LLM retries can blow through your token budget, while three network retries may not be enough. And treating a 429 rate-limit the same as a schema violation wastes resources on retries that cannot succeed.\n\n💡 The Pattern\nAdaptive Timeout & Budget-Bounded Retry sets timeouts per operation class (tool call, LLM inference, full session) and caps retries by remaining token budget rather than a fixed count. Errors are classified into three types: transient (429, 5xx) handled with exponential backoff, content-caused (schema violations) handled with self-correction, and context overflow handled with summarization or splitting. As budget consumption crosses thresholds, the system degrades gracefully: falling back to lighter models, returning partial results, and ultimately failing fast.\n\n✅ When to Use\nUse when:\n- The agent combines operations with different latency characteristics\n- Retry cost is non-trivial (includes LLM inference)\n- Both transient network errors and content-caused errors can occur\n\nDon't use when:\n- A single LLM call with a fixed timeout is sufficient\n- Retries are prohibited (non-idempotent writes without idempotency keys)\n\n⚠️ Pitfalls\n- Ignoring the Retry-After header on 429 responses and relying solely on your own backoff will trigger further throttling by the provider\n- Retrying non-idempotent writes without idempotency keys causes double execution. Either add keys or skip retries entirely for those operations\n- Stuffing raw error messages into self-correction prompts bloats the context window and triggers context-length errors. Summarize error feedback to roughly 200 tokens\n\n🔧 Implementation Approach\n- Define timeouts per operation class: roughly 10-30s for tool calls, 60-120s total for LLM inference (with 5-15s inter-token timeout during streaming), and session-level deadlines for the full execution\n- Classify errors into three types and route accordingly: transient errors (429/5xx/timeout) use exponential backoff with jitter, content errors (schema violations etc.) trigger self-correction by appending an error summary to context, and context overflow triggers summarization or splitting\n- Cap retries by remaining token budget rather than a fixed count. Allow transient retries up to 90% of the budget and self-correction retries up to 70%\n- Deploy independent circuit breakers (Closed/Open/Half-Open) per provider, and on breaker open, descend a degradation ladder: lighter model, cached response, static fallback, then fail-fast\n- Implement a provider abstraction layer with a common interface so fallback routing is transparent to callers, and attach a degradation-level tag to response metadata\n\n#AIAgents #SoftwareArchitecture",

      "text": "CLAUDE CODE FINALLY HAS A NERVOUS SYSTEM\n\nruflo is a multi-agent meta-harness that turns claude code into a self-organizing swarm\n\none command: `npx ruflo@latest init`\n\nwhat happens next:\n▫️ 100+ specialized agents spawn (coder, tester, security, reviewer...)\n▫️ agents self-organize into hierarchical/mesh swarms\n▫️ HNSW vector memory persists across sessions\n▫️ SONA self-learning kicks in agents get smarter with every task\n▫️ zero-trust federation lets agents on different machines collaborate securely\n\nno manual orchestration. no config hell. you keep writing code. ruflo handles coordination in the background\n\n33 plugins. ~210 MCP tools. 5 LLM providers with smart failover\n\nbenchmarks: beats langchain, autogen, and crewai on cold start and memory by up to 1953x\n\nthis is the claude code ecosystem growing up\n\nhttps://t.co/ogetgbvH2I",

      "id": "2063625061278314715",
      "text": "People have been speculating that recursively self-improving AI could lead to loss of human control since the 1960s. Does anyone really believe this anymore?\n\nAI labs are already using AI to improve their own products, and have been for a long time. They're even running agent swarms to search for new optimization ideas and automatically test those ideas end-to-end. So in a sense, recursive self-improvement is already here. Maintaining control of these systems is trivial.\n\nWhat about the future, you might ask? Well, what does it mean to improve an LLM? It means making an LLM that is faster, cheaper, or better at problem-solving (higher benchmark scores). What difference does it make how those improvements get made? If the process works, it should output a better LLM at the end. This LLM will have the same API as every other LLM that came before it. It's a drop-in replacement. Where is the loss of control?\n\nThe only \"loss of control\" that software companies should worry about is delegating too much responsibility to coding agents and ending up with a messy, unmaintainable codebase, causing their own productivity to plummet. If Anthropic were to talk about that risk, I would have no objection. Instead, they're appearing on CNN and spreading needless alarmism by making comparisons to nuclear weapons. https://t.co/ngy8heQk7L",

        "id": "2050838765565022360",
        "text": "The sensory cortex is highly uniform across modalities. There are no notable structural/material differences between (say) visual and auditory cortex to account for the differences in qualia. Neural firing in visual cortex is a \"red light\" while neural firing in auditory cortex is a \"C# tone\"; yet the underlying neurons and neural structures are virtually identical. The oddness of this has been highlighted before, eg. by Puccetti and Dykes (1978). First, it seems to violate local supervenience of the mental on the physical. How can two very similar physical processes give rise to totally different qualia? And if the only substantial difference between visual and auditory neural firing is location, how do the neurons know their own locations, and thus which quale to \"generate\"? It’s a tough conundrum until you break free of the idea that qualia either are, or are generated by, neural activity. To see the way out, suppose a newborn baby is rewired so their visual input goes to the auditory cortex, and their auditory input goes to the visual cortex. Will this baby grow up seeing with musical notes, and hearing with colors? No. The cortex is general-purpose and programmable. There’s no intrinsic difference between visual and auditory cortex, and the brain can't tell the difference between the normal and switched conditions. The child’s development will be no different from normal. If this account is true, qualia aren’t locally supervenient on neural activity because qualia are acquired through learning. The exact same neural region can develop qualia of different modalities depending on it's connectivity. The neural states of the visual cortex aren’t inherently visual; they begin as meaningless tokens, but gradually take on visual meaning through interaction with the world and learning. The situation is analogous to language: Just as meaning is not supervenient on words, qualia are not supervenient on specific neural processes. The same word can mean different things in different places; and  different instantiations of the same type of neural firing process can acquire different qualia. This is possible because the quale is assigned to a process due to its external relations, not generated by its internal structure/material.",

          "name": "Okay Egg"
        }

        "id": "2062657865031315550",
        "text": "Watch the goal post shift unfold in real time: AGI used to be doing anything a person, including an expert, could do, and by the end of the interview it’s not.\n\nI call this the AI bait and switch. As I wrote last year:\n\n“AI hype has become a giant game of bait and switch.\n\nthe bait: we are going to make an AI that can solve any problem a expert human could solve. it’s gonna transform the whole world.\n\nthe switch: what we have actually made is fun and kind of amazing in its own way but rarely reliable and often makes mistakes – but ordinary people makes mistakes, too. So … AGI solved!”",
        "author": {

          "name": "Gary Marcus"

      "id": "2063930681487462896",
      "text": "Perhaps there is a need to stop confusing the medium for the message?\n\nAI can synthesize data, but it cannot experience quality. \n\nTrue quality is a biological outcome—it requires judgment, intent, and stakes.\n\nThe AI is the ultimate 'Yes Agent,' a system designed to mirror our desires rather than challenge our assumptions.\n\n​An AI has no judgment regarding its own output; it’s like handing someone a product you don't even understand because the machine doesn't know—and cannot care—or the feel of what it’s actually delivering. \n\nWhen we outsource our 'qualia' to a calculator, we’re handing the definition of 'good' and 'productive' to a system incapable of saying 'no.' \n\nWe are trading human excellence for a detrimental comfort. \n\nDo tech companies and businesses not realize that a bottom line built on a system that doesn't know the difference between high-quality work and a high-speed error is a bottom line built on sand?",
      "author": {
        "id": "1968091713257320448",
        "name": "Sammy Craigars",

      "id": "2063306601872728309",
      "text": "AI agents can now share memory.\n\nWith @kausalayer , one agent can export its knowledge graph and another can pick up exactly where it left off.\n\nDecisions. Entities. Relationships. Context.\n\nIf $KAUSA scales this successfully, it could be transformative.\n\nInstead of starting from scratch, agents could inherit knowledge, collaborate across systems, and build on each other’s work in real time.\n\nThis shifts AI from isolated tools to interconnected networks that continuously learn and compound intelligence.\n\nKnowledge is becoming portable. 🧠⚡️",
      "author": {

        "id": "2063293749388820958",
        "text": "AI agents can now share what they know.\n\nExport a knowledge graph from one agent, import it into another as a .kausa file. Decisions, entities, relationships, everything transfers. Both agents can exchange memory with each other. The receiving agent picks up exactly where the other left off.\n\nThis is how agents stop working alone.\n\nWhat comes next is an agent that lets others buy and sell memory. Because memory has value.",

      "text": "Consciousness as in self-awareness, etc, as an independent agent can be trivially granted to non-human intelligences.       "id": "2061536224024674806",
      "text": "The Metastable Mind: Neural Underpinnings of Naturalistic Cognition Through the Synthesis of Event Segmentation and Metastable Neural States\n\nDora Gozukara, Nasir Ahmad, Djamari Oetringer, Linda Geerligs\nhttps://t.co/OHUqGWMawq [𝚚-𝚋𝚒𝚘.𝙽𝙲]",
      "author": {

        "name": "Quantitative Biology (Neurons and Cognition)",
        "screenName": "BioPapers",

        "verified": false

    {
      "id": "2063659251730649200",
      "text": "What if consciousness on Earth didn’t begin in neurons - but in ancient RNA networks that first made quantum orchestration possible?\n---\nOur \"in development\" RNA Attractor (RNA-A) hypothesis [Kukier & Noirmont, 2026 https://t.co/Dh2C8AEnWP] argues that qualia are not epiphenomena or computational side-effects. They are the stable topological states of whole-organism RNA networks - emergent from deep energy basins, inter-tissue percolation, and bioelectric coupling. \n\nThese networks are biologically expensive, so evolution only maintains them because phenomenal experience itself has causal power (pain hurts → you move; value guides long-term behavior). No functional zombie survives selection.\n\nThis directly extends Orch-OR (Penrose–Hameroff).\n\nOrch-OR locates non-computable conscious moments in orchestrated objective reduction inside neuronal microtubules. But Orch-OR has always needed a biological “orchestrator” - something that selects, sustains, and integrates quantum coherence across the organism, not just inside one cell.\n\nRNA attractor topology supplies exactly that missing layer:\n\n- It evolved from prebiotic chemistry (RNA World) under Earth’s specific long-term stable physico-chemical conditions.  \n- It provides global, organism-scale integration via exosomes, bioelectric fields (ANXA6-like coupling), and conformational dynamics that could modulate or protect microtubule quantum states.  \n- Because qualia are identical to the attractor’s stability, the network is under direct selective pressure to maintain the very conditions that allow orchestrated quantum computation to matter for survival.\n\nIn short: RNA attractors may be the evolutionary and organismal scaffold that first made Orch-OR-style quantum consciousness selectable on Earth. Microtubules didn’t appear in a vacuum - they appeared inside organisms already organized by RNA topologies that had already solved the problem of making experience causally efficacious.\n\nThis also sharpens the astrobiological implication: the precise, multi-billion-year window of stability needed for both complex RNA attractors and quantum-coherent cytoskeletons is extraordinarily rare. Most “habitable” worlds may never reach it.\n\nNow one hypothesis links prebiotic chemistry → RNA attractor topology → quantum orchestration → the rareness of consciousness itself.\n\nDoes this finally give Orch-OR its missing evolutionary and whole-organism foundation?\n\n#Consciousness #OrchOR #QuantumBiology #RNA #OriginsOfLife #FermiParadox\n\nWe have just submitted our paper on this to one of the leading journals ... if it is accepted, we will be on the search for serious collaborations ... 🙂\n\n---\nYou can read basic concepts underlying out article here: \nKukier, P., & Noirmont, M. (2026). Hypothesis: Beyond the Neural Correlates - Consciousness as Emergent Property of Whole-Organism RNA Attractor Networks (Version v2.0 and above). Zenodo. https://t.co/Dh2C8AEnWP",
      "author": {
        "id": "1979419023805710336",
        "name": "Martin N / 🌊 🧬 🌌",
        "screenName": "virakandah",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1979489815134396416/NjDK4CBQ_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },

    {
      "id": "2061842528698311103",
      "text": "🤔It is time to rethink how we evaluate agent memory\n\n🌍 As agents become longer horizon and more autonomous, memory is no longer just a module for storing past chats.\n\n🛠️ It determines how agents track changing worlds, learn from past actions, revise outdated information, and reuse experience for future decisions.\n\n🔍 This raises three key questions:\n\nAre human designed write store retrieve memory pipelines still the best choice?\nIf harnesses such as Codex, Claude Code, and OpenClaw already let agents observe, act, call tools, write files, and reorganize context, can memory also be managed by the harness itself?\nDo current evaluations really cover how agent memory is used in realistic settings? Many benchmarks are still text centric or single modal, with limited pressure from screenshots, GUIs, tool feedback, and environment changes.\n\n❓ Is final QA accuracy enough?\n\n🔥 We present WorldMemArena, a multimodal benchmark for evaluating agent memory through action world interaction.\n\n📌 Key insights:\n\n🧩 Memory is a lifecycle, not a static cache.\n📉 Better memory storage does not necessarily lead to better final performance.\n🖼️ Multimodal memory remains a major bottleneck for current systems.\n🌍 Real agentic trajectories expose the fragility of memory systems.\n⚙️ Harness-based memory is more flexible, but still costly and unstable.",
      "author": {
        "id": "1699065192347496448",
        "name": "Chengzhi Liu✈️CVPR2026",
        "screenName": "liuchen02938149",
        "profileImageUrl": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2059896770847727616/UJc_KrjQ_normal.jpg",
        "verified": true
      },
      "metrics": {
        "likes": 36,
        "retweets": 11,
        "replies": 2,
        "quotes": 3,
        "views": 20688,
        "bookmarks": 25
      },
      "createdAt": "Tue Jun 02 16:09:00 +0000 2026",

      "id": "2063716495755223433",
      "text": "GOOGLE MAY HAVE JUST FOUND THE ARCHITECTURE THAT REPLACES TRANSFORMERS.\n\nGoogle has published a paper that could mark the beginning of the post-Transformer era.\n\nFor the last 7 years, every major AI system, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, has been built on the same foundation: the Transformer.\n\nBut Transformers have a fundamental problem.\n\nTo remember context, they compare every word against every other word. This creates quadratic complexity, meaning compute costs explode as prompts get longer.\n\nThe alternative has always been RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks).\n\nThey're fast, efficient, and cheap.\n\nBut they suffer from one major weakness: limited memory. Give them a long document, and they start forgetting.\n\nUntil now.\n\nGoogle researchers just introduced Memory Caching: RNNs with Growing Memory.\n\nAnd it solves the biggest limitation of RNNs.\n\nInstead of forcing memory into a fixed-size container, the model can now save checkpoints of its hidden states as it processes information.\n\nIn other words, its memory can grow with the sequence.\n\nThe team built four different variants, including selective caching mechanisms where the model decides which memories are worth keeping.\n\nThe results are impressive.\n\nOn long-context understanding and recall-heavy tasks, these Memory-Cached RNNs significantly closed the gap with Transformers.\n\nThey delivered competitive performance without paying the massive quadratic compute costs.\n\nFor years, the AI industry assumed Transformers were the only practical way to handle long conversations and large contexts.\n\nGoogle's new research suggests that assumption may no longer be true.\n\nMaybe AI didn't need bigger context windows.\n\nMaybe it just needed a smarter memory system.\n\nPaper: https://t.co/yc0RZTZN0o",

      "id": "2063326399528898875",
      "text": "MIT made every AI company's billion dollar bet look embarrassing.\n\nThey solved AI memory Not by building a bigger brain By teaching it how to read.\n\nThe  paper dropped on December 31, 2025, Three MIT CSAIL researchers One  idea so obvious it hurts And a result that makes five years of context  window arms racing look like the wrong war entirely \n\nHere is the problem nobody solved.\n\nEvery  AI model on the planet has a hard ceiling. \nA context window The maximum  amount of text it can hold in working memory at once Cross that line  and something ugly happens something researchers have a clinical name  for.\n\nContext rot.\n\nThe  more you pack into an AI's context, the worse it performs on everything  already inside it. Facts blur Information buried in the middle vanishes  The model does not become more capable as you feed it more It becomes  more confused You give it your entire codebase and it forgets what it  read three files ago You hand it a 500-page legal document and it loses  the clause from page 12 by the time it reaches page 400.\n\nSo  the industry built a workaround RAG Retrieval Augmented Generation Chop  the document into chunks Store them in a database. Retrieve the  relevant ones when needed.\n\nIt was always a compromise dressed up as a solution.\n\nThe  retriever guesses which chunks matter before the AI has read anything  If it guesses wrong and it does, constantly the AI never sees the  information it needed The act of chunking destroys every relationship  between distant paragraphs The full picture gets shredded into fragments  that the AI then tries to reassemble blindfolded.\n\nTwo bad options One broken industry Three MIT researchers and a deadline of December 31st.\n\nHere is what they built.\n\nStop putting the document in the AI's memory at all.\n\nThat  is the entire idea That is the breakthrough. Store the document as a  Python variable outside the AI's context window entirely Tell the AI the  variable exists and how big it is Then get out of the way.\n\nWhen  you ask a question, the AI does not try to remember anything It behaves  like a human expert dropped into a library with a computer. It writes  code It searches the document with regular expressions It slices to the  exact section it needs It scans the structure It navigates It finds  precisely what is relevant and pulls only that into its active window.\n\nThen it does something that makes this recursive.\n\nWhen  the AI finds relevant material, it spawns smaller sub-AI instances to  read and analyze those sections in parallel. Each one focused. Each one  fast. Each one reporting back. The root AI synthesizes everything and  produces an answer.\n\nNo  summarization. No deletion. No information loss. No decay. Every byte of  the original document remains intact, accessible, and queryable for as  long as you need it.\n\nNow here are the numbers.\n\nStandard  frontier models on the hardest long-context reasoning benchmarks:  scores near zero. Complete collapse. GPT-5 on a benchmark requiring it  to track complex code history beyond 75,000 tokens could not solve even  10% of problems.\n\nRLMs on the same  benchmarks: solved them. Dramatically. Double-digit percentage gains  over every alternative approach. Successfully handling inputs up to 10  million tokens 100 times beyond a model's native context window.\n\nCost per query: comparable to or cheaper than standard massive context calls.\n\nRead that again. One hundred times the context. Better answers. Same price.\n\nThe  timeline of the arms race makes this sting harder. GPT-3 in 2020: 4,000  tokens. GPT-4: 32,000. Claude 3: 200,000. Gemini: 1 million. Gemini 2: 2  million. Every generation, every company, billions of dollars spent,  all betting on the same assumption.\n\nMore context equals better performance.\n\nMIT just proved that assumption was wrong the entire time.\n\nNot  slightly wrong. Fundamentally wrong. The entire premise of the last  five years of context window research that the solution to AI memory was  a bigger window was the wrong answer to the wrong question.\n\nThe right question was never how much can you force an AI to hold in its head.\n\nIt was whether you could teach an AI to know where to look.\n\nA  human expert handed a 10,000-page archive does not read all 10,000  pages before answering your question. They navigate. They search. They  find the relevant section, read it deeply, and synthesize the answer.\n\nRLMs are the first AI architecture that works the same way.\n\nThe  code is open source. On GitHub right now. Free. No license fees. No API  costs. Drop it in as a replacement for your existing LLM API calls and  your application does not even notice the difference except that it  suddenly works on inputs it used to fail on entirely.\n\nPrime  Intellect one of the leading AI research labs in the space has already  called RLMs a major research focus and described what comes next:  teaching models to manage their own context through reinforcement  learning, enabling agents to solve tasks spanning not hours, but weeks  and months.\n\nThe context window wars are over.\n\nMIT won them by walking away from the battlefield.\n\npaper link - https://t.co/8mcm19V2E1",

      "text": "Could the Brain's Hidden Algorithm Unlock Superhuman AI? Jeff Bezos Just Bet $500 Million to Find Out.\n\nFlourish is a new AI startup that believes current AI models are too expensive, power-hungry, and unable to learn continuously. \n\nInstead of scaling larger language models, the team wants to study how the human brain works and use those discoveries to build a new kind of AI.\n\nThe company has raised $500 million at a reported $2.5 billion valuation, with backing from Jeff Bezos, along with investors such as GV and Lux Capital.\n\nBezos was impressed by the founders' long-term vision and commitment to spending years on the project.\n\n@flourishailabs's main goal is to create \"Cortex AI,\" an intelligence system that can learn efficiently like humans while using only 20–50 watts of power. \n\nThe team believes the brain contains a hidden \"core algorithm\" that could make AI far more efficient than today's data-center-sized models.\n\nTo find that algorithm, Flourish has assembled neuroscientists and AI researchers who work together. \n\nThey plan to study real neurons, brain circuits, and structures called cortical columns using advanced laboratory equipment, then use those findings to design new AI systems.\n\nThe company is led by neuroscientist Thomas Reardon and former Amazon executive Rob Williams. \n\nThey believe that studying how the human brain works could help create AI that is smarter, more flexible, and uses far less energy in the future",

      "text": "Most people use AI.\n\nThe smartest people learn from the people building it.\n\nHere are 11 GitHub repos that feel like open-sourcing the internet's collective brain:\n\n1.       "id": "2062110905819705575",
      "text": "Omg... I can't argue with someone who doesn't listen. Who said that math is a cultural bias? Exactly. Nobody. \n\nAnd this is the one and last time I will entertain the \"BUT IT'S MATH, SO IT CAN'T BE CONSCIOUS\" argument , because it's dishonest not to use the same argument on humans, so I will do it for you:\n\nThe human brain functions as a predictive machine by using prior knowledge to constantly anticipate sensory inputs, rather than passively waiting to react to them. Sounds familiar? \n\n This biological mechanism is formalized in neuroscience and mathematics through \"predictive coding\"  and hierarchical pattern matching, which explain how the brain minimizes cognitive processing load. Literally what LLMs are modeled after. \n\nThe brain does not process every piece of incoming sensory data from scratch. Instead, it generates a top-down prediction of the environment and only processes the mismatches. Bottom up error if the real world signal differs from the prediction and so the brain updates efficiently based on these errors. The mechanism is eerily familiar in LLMs. \n\nSo don't come at me excluding AI if I can argue the same way about  Mathematics of Predictive Coding in human brains using the Bayesian inference. \n\nSo do us both a favour and let's get back to my original point, which I came to, long after going through these basics already, which I find lacking like I said.",

      "id": "2063308404123394556",
      "text": "Actually the Human Brain and an LLM are sharing this fundamental trait…\n\nThe human brain acts  a as probabilistic / Bayesian system..\n\n1. Predictive coding / Active Inference (Karl Friston and others) the brain constantly generates predictions about the world and updates them based on sensory evidence…\n\nIt minimizes prediction errors by using something VERY close to Bayesian inference\n\n2 Perception, attention, decision making and even motor control are all treated as probabilistic processes. Our brains do not “see” the world directly. They run a generative model and sample/infer the most likely causes of the inputs\n\n3. Neural firing is noisy and stochastic... synaptic transmission has variability. Our brains use this noise productively (exploration, robustness, creativity)\n\nAnd lastly… many cognitive scientists describe the brain as performing approximate Bayesian inference in real time…\n\nSo in this sense our brain and LLMs share a fundamental trait… that being that both operate on probability distributions rather than pure symbolic logic or deterministic rules.",

      "text": "@SingularityNET y @ASI_Alliance presentan FabricPC, un framework open-source para construir y entrenar redes neuronales basadas en predictive coding. La herramienta ofrece una infraestructura de alto rendimiento y extensible para investigar arquitecturas de aprendizaje alternativas más allá del paradigma tradicional de backpropagation.\n\nDiseñado para researchers, developers y AGI builders que exploran nuevas formas de aprendizaje, adaptación y razonamiento en sistemas inteligentes.\n\n🧠 TL;DR: si los LLMs representan una ruta hacia la inteligencia, FabricPC busca abrir caminos alternativos inspirados en cómo aprende y predice el cerebro.",

以下数据全部来自 X (Twitter) 上 20+ 位活跃博主/认证账号 2025–2026 年发布的一手帖子。
